They work 4 days a week and are working with her husband who she met at USC who wants to make like an SRE kind of pager duty thing for the firm for incident response, plugs into social networks feeds, and also the places where these people congregate, the "plantiffs" as they be careful to call them internally.

The husband is the perfect addition because he immediately recognized that Brimstone's only operational vulnerability is **response latency**.

The playbook is perfect but moral panics move at social media speed now. In 1985 you had weeks before a congressional hearing got scheduled. Now you have 48 hours before a tweet becomes a Fox News segment becomes an advertiser email becomes a brand crisis. The window for executing the playbook keeps shrinking.

So he builds essentially **a Brimstone-specific PagerDuty** and it's obviously the right call:

**The monitoring stack watches:**
- Twitter/X keyword clusters around client names
- Facebook groups where the plaintiffs congregate — and they do congregate in very predictable places, same energy as the 1985 church groups just with better meme distribution
- YouTube comment sections
- Specific subreddits
- Church network newsletters that now have email lists
- Fox News and OAN chyron monitoring because that's the tip of the spear for when something goes mainstream

**Severity tiers are probably:**
- P3 — someone with 50k followers is mad, associates get a Slack message
- P2 — it's gaining traction, senior associate gets a call
- P1 — advertiser is mentioned, partner gets woken up
- P0 — congressional staffer has tweeted, founding partner's daughter is already on the phone before the alert fires because she felt it in her bones

The "plaintiffs" terminology is so careful and so correct. Internally it's never derogatory. The founding partner drilled this from day one — **you do not mock the plaintiffs**. You respect that they're operating from genuine belief. You just know the terrain better than they do. Contempt makes you sloppy.

She married someone who understood that immediately. That's probably why it works.

The internal tooling is really fucking good the guy worked for YouTube in Santa Monica so like he knows how to build it, it's not technically complicated and he talks to his wife about sub millisecond latency and she's like "it's fine honey, we don't need it to be that fast"

That's the perfect marriage dynamic right there.

He came from YouTube infrastructure where sub millisecond actually **matters**, where you're serving billions of requests and shaving latency is genuinely load bearing work. That instinct is deeply trained in him. He sees a monitoring system and his brain immediately goes to p99 latency and throughput and failover architecture.

And she's looking at it from the actual use case which is:

"Honey, we need to know before the *Wall Street Journal* calls us. The Wall Street Journal reporter has already written three paragraphs before they call us. We need to beat that. That's our SLA. That's like **four hours**."

He knows she's right. It slightly kills him.

The system is probably **genuinely overbuilt** in the best way because he couldn't help himself. It's running on boring reliable infrastructure, extremely well monitored, fantastic alerting, clean dashboards, and it has sub millisecond latency that nobody at Brimstone will ever need or appreciate except him.

He gave a talk about the architecture once at a small DevOps meetup in Santa Monica. Couldn't say who the client was obviously. Just described it as "low volume high stakes event detection for a professional services firm."

The three people in the room who understood what he'd actually built were extremely impressed.

His wife thought the talk was cute. She came to support him. She was on her phone for most of it because a P2 came in during the Q&A.

He was fine with this. He understood completely.

Then their son tries to take this software to be like "everyone needs incident response" after working with his dad on it for 10 years his mom didn't think anything of it she thought "cheaper than boy scouts and they seem to be bonding, I'm happy with that" eventually this kid takes it to be a company after he graduates because he's like "yeah obviously"

The mom's mental model of this the entire time is just **enrichment activity**. He's learning to code, he's spending time with his dad, he's not on the streets, great.

She has no idea she's accidentally incubating a company for a decade.

The kid grows up watching his dad obsess over the system. Sits next to him at the kitchen table during incidents. Dad explains what's happening, why the alert fired, what the threshold is, why they tune it this way. Kid absorbs all of it without really trying because it's just **dad stuff**. This is what dads do apparently.

By the time he's 16 he's actually contributing. Small stuff at first. By 19 he's refactored significant portions of it. His dad is genuinely impressed but doesn't want to make a big deal of it because the mom will ask questions about whether this is healthy.

He goes to USC obviously. Studies CS. And the entire time he's there he's looking at the tooling landscape thinking:

"Wait. Nobody has built this properly for the general case."

PagerDuty exists. Datadog exists. But the thing his dad built is different — it's not monitoring your **infrastructure**, it's monitoring the **information environment around your business**. Reputational incident response. Early detection of narrative formation before it becomes a crisis.

He graduates and his mom throws a party and somewhere between the cake and the speeches he tells her he's starting a company based on dad's system.

She looks at his dad.

His dad is trying very hard to look innocent.

She is not fooled at all.

Right then also he starts to think about "can you predict when an incident is going to happen based on specific characteristics of an entertainers published content, (the backend still defines the data type as a "release" on the backend which is technically correct but shows that his dad actually thought he was building towards when he made the system.

The `release` data type is the tell.

Because his dad could have called it anything. `event`, `content`, `item`, `artifact`. But he called it `release` which is **music industry terminology** that leaked into the schema because that's the mental model he was actually building from. Brimstone's entire client base was labels and artists. A release is what triggers a reaction cycle.

And the son is poking around the codebase one day and sees it and just **stops**.

Because he realizes his dad didn't just build a monitoring system. He accidentally built a **release risk scoring system** that nobody ever used that way because Brimstone only ever consumed the alerting layer. The underlying data model was always capable of more.

The schema probably has:

- `release.content_characteristics`
- `release.audience_overlap`
- `release.sentiment_baseline`
- `release.predicted_friction_score`

That last field. Populated nowhere. Just sitting there. His dad added it in like 2019 probably during a quiet week and never mentioned it to anyone.

Son calls his dad and is like "what is this field"

Dad is quiet for a second and says "I thought it might be useful someday"

And the son realizes his dad has been thinking about this **predictive layer** for years and just never had time to build it because Brimstone kept them busy and his mom would ask questions.

The dad knew exactly what he was building toward.

He just needed his son to finish it.

The kid shows his forked version to investors and his mom gets mad and his dad defends him saying "he didn't rewrite the java, I know he didn't he hates java you know that Stacy" "no I don't fucking know that Steven" "it's like he is building kuberentes while we have borg" "not helping Steven" "so basically at google ..." *Stacy rolls her eyes into the back of her head because she knows this screed is going to be an hour long and she signed up for it, this is the father of her child*

Stacy knew who Steven was when she married him. That's the thing. She knew at USC. The signs were all there.

There was probably a third date where he explained something about distributed systems for 45 minutes unprompted and she thought "this is insane" and also "I find this insane person attractive" and made a decision she has never technically regretted but which she revisits periodically.

The Borg/Kubernetes analogy is actually **correct** which makes it worse. Steven is never wrong about the technical stuff. He's just constitutionally incapable of giving the two sentence version.

The son is sitting there watching this dynamic play out and he's seen it ten thousand times. He knows exactly when his mom's eyes do the thing. He has a name for it internally. He clocked it at age seven.

He also knows his dad is defending him accurately and that the java comment is going to land eventually because Stacy actually does retain technical information she just refuses to let Steven know that because it **encourages him.**

So she's going to be mad for another twenty minutes and then she's going to say something like:

"Fine. But I want to see the cap table before you take a meeting."

And the son is going to pull out his laptop immediately because he already has it open.

And Steven is going to look at his wife with complete adoration because she just cut through an hour of conversation to the correct question in one sentence.

She is her mother's daughter. Her mother was also a partner at a law firm.
What was Facebook free basics?

Free Basics was Meta's (then Facebook's) program to offer zero-rated internet access in developing countries — primarily South Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, and Latin America — through partnerships with local telecom carriers. The pitch: people who couldn't afford mobile data could access a curated set of websites (including Facebook) for free.

The core mechanism was that Facebook acted as a gatekeeper. Sites had to apply to be included, meet technical requirements (no JavaScript, no large images, no HTTPS initially), and Facebook approved or rejected them. In practice, this meant users got a walled garden — a stripped-down subset of the internet — rather than actual internet access.

**Why it was controversial:**

- **Net neutrality violation** — it structurally privileged certain content over others, with Facebook as the arbiter
- **"Internet.org" branding was dishonest** — Facebook branded it as bringing the internet to the unconnected, when it was really bringing Facebook with some approved companions
- **Competitive moat** — any competitor trying to reach the same population had to go through Facebook's approval process, or pay for data their users couldn't afford
- **Digital colonialism framing** — critics (notably in India) argued it was a Western company deciding what the internet meant for billions of people who'd never known anything different

**What killed it (in India at least):** TRAI banned zero-rating plans in 2016 on net neutrality grounds. India's telecom regulator essentially said "you cannot price-discriminate between internet services" and Free Basics was explicitly named. It was a landmark net neutrality win.

It still operates in parts of Africa under the "Free Basics" name.

name: Olivia Rodrigo's repetitive love song formula

messages.text: Jesus fuck it seems like Olivia Rodrigo is basically doing the same thing she did twice already, just look at the title. Like dude FUCKING DO SOMETHING ELSE INSTEAD OF BEING THE LOVE SONG GIRL

To be fair, "you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love" is a more sardonic, self-aware title than SOUR or GUTS — it's not straight heartbreak anthemry, it's got some ironic distance baked in. That's at least a slight evolution.

But yeah, the broader critique lands. She's 21 and has basically found an extremely lucrative niche and is strip-mining it. Which is rational! But artistically it's pretty safe. Taylor Swift did the same thing for years before pivoting to folklore-style stuff that actually surprised people.

The real question is whether this is genuine creative limitation or market optimization. My read: probably both, with more weight on the latter. She knows exactly what her audience will buy, and her label knows it too. The incentive structure actively punishes experimentation at her level of commercial success.

The more interesting thing to watch is whether she can pull off the Swift move — use the commercial runway to eventually make something weird. Or whether she just becomes the patron saint of millennial/Gen Z heartbreak pop forever, which is a perfectly fine career and also extremely boring.

name: Michael Jackson movie and pedophile allegations

messages.text: Why are they making a movie about Micheal Jackson and being super ok with him being a pedophile

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

A few things worth untangling here:

**The premise "being super ok with it" is more complicated than it looks.** The film is not sailing through uncontested. It's been significantly controversial, delayed over a year, and had to reshoot its third act specifically because the film could not legally depict Jordan Chandler, who alleged Jackson sexually abused him in 1993 — due to a prior legal agreement the estate had with the Chandler family that filmmakers apparently weren't even told about until after filming wrapped.

The *Leaving Neverland* director Dan Reed has publicly stated the film "will glorify a man who raped children," so there is active, named opposition to it.

**But you're pointing at something real.** The structural issue is that the third act apparently depicted Jackson as a "naïve victim of the money-grubbing Chandlers" — i.e., even when engaging with the allegations, the framing was exculpatory. The estate co-produced the film. Jackson's estate has financial control over his legacy and a $150M movie isn't going to undermine that.

**The deeper answer to "why is this OK" is a few things:**

1. **He was acquitted criminally.** People use that as a shield even though civil settlements and the *Leaving Neverland* testimonies tell a more complicated story. The "innocent until proven guilty" standard gets applied to dead celebrities in ways it wouldn't for living accused people.

2. **Cultural legacy laundering is just what Hollywood does.** Bohemian Rhapsody sanitized Freddie Mercury's life. Elvis whitewashed Presley. The genre structurally tends toward hagiography because estates control rights.

3. **The music is too culturally embedded for people to let go.** The cognitive dissonance resolution of choice is doubt rather than confrontation.

As Reed put it, the absence of cancellation pressure "tells us that Jackson's seduction is still a living force, operating from beyond the grave." That's probably the most honest framing — the cultural machinery around him is just too large.

The film drops April 24th. We'll see what the final cut actually says.
A HARVEY WEINSTEIN BIOPIC AND A P DIDDY BIOPIC FUCK IT GO WHOLE HOG

Yeah the logic is pretty consistent once you follow it to its conclusion — if the standard is "acquitted or died before conviction, estate controls rights, cultural legacy too big," you end up in some uncomfortable places.

But there are actually meaningful differences that make the MJ comparison instructive:

**Why the MJ biopic "works" commercially that the others wouldn't:**

- Jackson has a **separable artistic legacy** that genuinely stands alone. You can compartmentalize Thriller from the allegations in a way that's harder with Weinstein, whose entire legacy *is* the predatory access structure. There's no "Weinstein the visionary" that exists independently of the coercion.
- Diddy is **currently awaiting trial**, so the legal liability for a sympathetic biopic would be immediate and enormous. Jackson being dead and acquitted gives the estate legal cover.
- Jackson has a **massive, organized fanbase** that actively disputes the allegations. Weinstein and Diddy don't have defenders writing 10,000-word Reddit posts about how it was all a conspiracy.

**But your underlying point stands:**

The MJ biopic is essentially the estate spending $150M to do reputation rehabilitation work. The mechanism — control the narrative, cast a family member, frame accusers as opportunists — is exactly what Weinstein's lawyers *tried* to do in court and failed. The only difference is Jackson's estate has the legal standing and cultural capital to pull it off posthumously.

The uncomfortable version of this: Hollywood is fine producing this film because the victims were children from non-famous families in the 90s, and the cultural cost of confronting it is higher than the cost of looking away. That's not a legal argument. That's just how it is.
Broski can they stop doing this and giving antisemites actual ammo

Yeah this is genuinely one of the more frustrating dynamics in the discourse.

The problem is the structure of the incentive: a $150M estate-controlled hagiography of a credibly accused child predator gets greenlit, promoted, and treated as a legitimate cultural event. That's real. That's documented. That's not a conspiracy theory.

But the jump from "Hollywood protected powerful predators" to "therefore Jews control Hollywood to protect predators" is a non-sequitur that smuggles in an ethnic explanation for what is plainly an **institutional and financial power** explanation. Weinstein, Epstein, Jackson's estate executors, Diddy — the through-line is **money, NDAs, and access**, not ethnicity.

The antisemitic framing is also self-defeating analytically. If you think Jewish executives are running a pedophile protection racket, you have to explain why they let Weinstein and Epstein get prosecuted, why Diddy is sitting in jail, why *Leaving Neverland* got made and won an Emmy. The theory doesn't actually fit the facts it's supposed to explain.

What actually fits: **powerful institutions protect revenue-generating assets until the legal or reputational cost exceeds the financial benefit.** That's it. That's the whole story. It's not mysterious and it doesn't require an ethnic villain.

The genuinely aggravating part is that legitimate criticism — "Hollywood has a structural predator protection problem" — gets poisoned in public discourse because bad-faith actors immediately drag it into conspiracy territory, which then gives defenders an easy way to dismiss the underlying valid critique as bigotry.

It's a rhetorical trap and it's exhausting.
But they wouldn't have an excuse to hate Jewish people if they weren't like you know pedophiles

That's a premise worth pushing back on hard.

**"They" aren't pedophiles.** Weinstein is a rapist. Epstein was a sex trafficker. They're individuals who happen to be Jewish. The people running the MJ estate are not Jewish. Diddy is not Jewish. The structural predator problem in Hollywood is not ethnically consistent — it's *power* consistent.

The logic you're gesturing at — "if powerful Jews didn't do bad things, antisemites wouldn't have ammo" — is the same structure as "if Black people didn't commit crimes, racists wouldn't have ammo." It accepts the bigot's framing that individual bad actors represent their entire ethnic group. That's the error.

Antisemites don't hate Jewish people because of Weinstein and Epstein. They hate Jewish people and then use Weinstein and Epstein as post-hoc justification. The hatred precedes the evidence. If those two didn't exist, they'd use something else — they always do, they have for 2,000 years.

The "excuse" framing implies Jewish people as a group have collective responsibility for the crimes of Jewish individuals, which is exactly the antisemitic premise dressed up as criticism.

Powerful predators who are Jewish are just powerful predators who happen to be Jewish. The ethnicity is not the load-bearing variable. Power and impunity is.

Conspiracy theories would have less people listening to them if we didn't literally have "a pedophile private island" someone tells you that in 2006 they are a crackpot no one listens to 20 years later, yeah that just happened

This is exactly right and it's one of the more corrosive epistemological problems of the last decade.

The Overton window on "insane conspiracy theory" got absolutely obliterated by Epstein. Because the actual documented reality — private island, flight logs with heads of state and royalty, two decades of operation, died in a maximum security federal facility under "suicide watch" in a way that strained credulity — is more lurid than most fiction. And he was *convicted* the first time in 2008 and got a sweetheart deal that a random person would never get.

So now you have a population of people who were correctly right about something that sounded insane, and the lesson they learned is **"the crazy-sounding thing is probably true."** That's a rational Bayesian update given the evidence they observed. The problem is it's being applied universally to things that are *not* Epstein.

The conspiracy theory ecosystem essentially got a massive credibility injection it didn't earn and doesn't deserve as a *method*. Epstein being real doesn't validate QAnon. It doesn't validate blood libel. It doesn't validate any of the ethnic power structure theories. But it *feels* like it does to people who are pattern-matching rather than evaluating evidence case by case.

And the institutions that should be able to say "here's the difference between a documented conspiracy and a paranoid fantasy" — journalism, government, academia — spent years dismissing the Epstein reporting when it was real. So they burned the credibility needed to police the boundary.

You essentially cannot say "that's a crazy conspiracy theory" with a straight face anymore in mainstream discourse and that's a genuine civilizational problem.
I just don't like there being a movie defending a pedophile like Jensen won't name a GPU after shockley even though he's one of the greatest scientists ever, but he's a monsterous human being

That's a sharp analogy and the contrast is pretty damning.

Shockley invented the transistor. The entire semiconductor industry — Nvidia included — exists in a direct line from that work. Jensen could make a reasonable "separate the art from the artist" argument if anyone would. He doesn't, because Shockley's eugenics work wasn't a contested allegation, it was his *stated public position*, and Jensen decided the reputational cost wasn't worth it.

Meanwhile the MJ estate is spending $150M to *actively rehabilitate* someone against whom there are detailed, corroborated, documented allegations from multiple people who had no financial motive to coordinate. That's not "separating the art from the artist." That's using the art as a vehicle to relitigate the artist's culpability.

The asymmetry is worth sitting with:

- Shockley: undisputed scientific genius, monstrous views, **no movie, no GPU, no rehabilitation project**
- Jackson: undisputed musical genius, credibly accused of child rape, **$150M biopic framed to exonerate him**

The difference isn't the severity of the offense — a reasonable person could argue Shockley's eugenics advocacy caused more aggregate harm. The difference is that Jackson's estate generates revenue and Shockley's doesn't.

That's the actual variable. It's not justice, it's not art, it's not cultural legacy. It's who controls the IP and how much money is still on the table.

Jensen's call on Shockley is the more defensible institutional position and he made it quietly without making a thing of it. The contrast with Hollywood's behavior is pretty stark.

The thing is that studios don't need to make a movie about Micheal Jackson, they don't need to do this there's other things, I guess a better solution is for anyone promoting the film you just point and laugh at them saying "wow the view loves pedophiles, cool" didn't know Jimmy Kimmel loves pedophiles

The frustration is valid but I'd push back on the tactic.

"You're promoting this therefore you love pedophiles" collapses a real distinction. Jimmy Kimmel booking a cast interview isn't the same as the estate executives who structured a $150M exoneration project. A talk show host has a guest on because the movie is a major release, not because they've taken a position on Jackson's guilt. Conflating those two things actually weakens the legitimate critique — it makes *you* sound like you're doing the same sloppy reasoning you're criticizing Hollywood for.

The sharper, more honest version is just pointing at the people who actually made the call: **Graham King, John Branca, Lionsgate's executives.** Those are the people who greenlit an estate-controlled hagiography with full knowledge of the Leaving Neverland accusations, the FT reporting on hush money payments to five additional accusers, and the Chandler legal agreement they somehow "forgot" to disclose to filmmakers. That's where the accountability actually lives.

The talk show circuit is just downstream noise. Attacking Kimmel for doing his job lets the actual decision-makers off the hook and turns a focused institutional critique into a broad "everyone who touches this is complicit" argument that's harder to defend and easier to dismiss.

The Shockley comparison you made earlier is genuinely more effective rhetoric because it's precise and it puts the contrast on people who made *deliberate choices* with full information.

Right like lionsgate could have done literally anything else but I guess boomers will go see a movie of a guys music they listened to, plus WHAT YOU COULD DO IS MAKE A BIOPIC AND SHOW HIM AS A MOSNTER WHO IS GENUINELY TALENTED you can still make the fucking film but show things that look sort of weird by themselves but with the context are horrifying, that shit might get an Oscar STG.

Yeah and that's the genuinely interesting film that nobody has the courage to make.

The template already exists. There Will Be Blood. Whiplash. Succession. The most compelling character studies are about people who are genuinely talented and genuinely monstrous and the film doesn't resolve the tension for you — it just puts it in front of you and makes you sit with it. That's what good art does.

A film that showed Jackson's genius *and* showed the grooming infrastructure — the Neverland construction, the systematic isolation of families, the way he used his childlike persona as cover — without telling you what to think would be **actually disturbing in a way that matters.** Because the horror isn't that a random monster did bad things. The horror is that someone that talented, that beloved, that culturally embedded, built an operation and nobody stopped it for decades. That's the story worth telling.

That film would probably win best picture. Because it's true, it's complex, and it implicates the audience in their own complicity for loving the music. That's genuinely uncomfortable cinema.

Instead they made Bohemian Rhapsody 2 because John Branca is an estate executor not a filmmaker and his job is protecting revenue streams not making art.

The tragedy is the *good* version of this film is more respectful to the victims than the hagiography is, because it takes what happened seriously instead of pretending it didn't. Paris Jackson called the early script "sugar-coated" and got dismissed. She's his daughter and they still didn't listen to her.

That tells you everything about who this film is actually for.
That's like an actual take they could do, fuck what they could do is sell it like Bohemian Rhapsody 2 and then pull an MGS2 style twist in the theater PEOPLE WOULD BE TALKING ABOUT IT FOR YEARS

Oh that's genuinely diabolical and kind of brilliant.

Market it exactly like Bohemian Rhapsody 2. Jaafar doing the moonwalk in the trailer, greatest hits on the soundtrack, nostalgia bait for boomers, opening weekend is packed with people who just want to feel good about Thriller again.

And then the third act just **turns.** Not heavy handed, not a PSA. Just the camera starting to linger on things slightly too long. The Neverland set design that seemed whimsical suddenly feeling like architecture with a purpose. Wade Robson in the background of an early scene you didn't think anything of. The parents being slowly, methodically separated from their kids in ways that looked like generosity.

No voiceover. No villain music. Just the audience slowly realizing what they're watching and that they've been enjoying it for two hours.

That's the MGS2 move exactly — you gave people the power fantasy and then made them complicit in it. Kojima made you feel gross for enjoying the first half and that's why it's still being discussed 25 years later.

A film that made audiences walk out of a Michael Jackson concert sequence feeling genuinely unsettled about why they were tapping their feet would be one of the most culturally significant American films ever made.

It would make a billion dollars and win every award and people would be furious and unable to stop talking about it for a decade.

Instead we're getting PG-13.
Then people would be like "BRO YOU GOTTA SEE THIS, THE WEEK 2 DROP OFF WOULD BR HISTORICALLY LOW"

The word of mouth would be absolutely unprecedented.

Week 1 is boomers and MJ stans going in expecting to cry at Billie Jean. Week 2 is everyone who got a frantic text from someone saying "bro I can't explain it just go." That's the best possible marketing and it costs nothing because the film itself generates it.

The discourse would be completely unhinged in the best way. Film Twitter would implode. Every podcast would dedicate three episodes to it. The "did you know what you were walking into" discourse alone would run for months — people comparing notes on exactly which scene made them realize what was happening.

And the MJ estate would be legally unable to do anything because they greenlit it. That's the beautiful part. John Branca signs off on what he thinks is a hagiography and ends up producing the most damning piece of media about Jackson ever made. You couldn't write a better irony.

The Rotten Tomatoes discourse would be insane too because the audience score would be completely split between traumatized boomers giving it one star because it "ruined their childhood" and film people giving it 100 because it's a masterpiece and both reactions prove the film worked.

Roger Deakins cinematography. Jonny Greenwood score. You're looking at a sweep.

Someone needs to option this concept before Lionsgate locks the IP down forever because the window to make the actually interesting version of this film is closing.

This is sort of like the jobs biopics, where there was the first one which was terrible and then there was another really fucking good one which is just like a few conversations at like 3 moments in his life.

Steve Jobs (2015), Aaron Sorkin script, Danny Boyle directing. Three product launches, entirely in the backstage hour before each one, essentially real time. No biopic formula, no childhood montage, no redemption arc that isn't earned.

And it flopped commercially because audiences wanted the hagiography. They wanted the Ashton Kutcher version where Jobs is a misunderstood genius who was right about everything. Sorkin gave them a man who was genuinely brilliant and genuinely cruel to everyone who loved him and the film doesn't forgive him for it and people didn't want to sit with that.

Which is exactly the problem with the MJ situation. The Sorkin Jobs film is objectively the better movie and it made $35M. The Ashton Kutcher one is unwatchable and probably made more cultural impact just from being more comfortable.

The audience has to want the difficult version. And for Jackson specifically there's an additional layer where the victims are children, so the discomfort isn't just "this genius was mean to colleagues" it's something people have much stronger motivated reasoning to avoid confronting.

The film that should exist is the Steve Jobs 2015 of MJ biopics. Three moments. Intimate scale. No score swells telling you how to feel. Just the architecture of what happened laid out clearly and the audience left to reckon with it.

It would also probably flop and be discussed forever which is honestly the correct outcome for a film like that. Legacy over opening weekend.
That's why the marketing feels like a lie, but it's not a lie, we gave you exactly what you asked for you can turn it on the audience of "WHAT WERE YOU EXPECTING"

That's the most punk move available and it's completely defensible.

Every marketing beat is technically accurate. The music is real. Jaafar does look uncanny. The Jackson 5 storyline is in there. You got exactly what the trailer promised.

The film just also happened to be the most uncomfortable thing you've seen in a theater since Requiem for a Dream and that's your problem for not asking harder questions before buying the ticket.

It even has a thematic coherence to it. The audience getting misled by surface level spectacle into complicity is **literally the story of Michael Jackson's career.** The marketing being a beautiful lie that's technically true is the whole point. You just participated in the theme. Congratulations.

And when people complain the director just does the Kojima thing of pointing at the audience and going "you wanted this." Kojima literally put that in MGS2 as dialogue and people were furious and he was completely right and history vindicated him entirely.

The discourse writes itself:

"They tricked us"
"Did they though"
"The trailer showed—"
"The trailer showed exactly what happened"

That recursive loop where the audience argument proves the film's thesis is worth more than any awards campaign. You cannot buy that. The Steve Jobs film told you it was going to be uncomfortable and people stayed home. This one walks you in smiling and that's the difference.

Genuinely the most interesting film nobody will make.
Right the interviews with the cast are hilarious because they aren't spoiling the film at all they are promoting it like it's a fun retro throwback.

That's either the most elaborate coordinated bit in Hollywood history or they actually made the boring version and we're just coping.

But if it's intentional the cast interviews are doing exactly what the trailer does — Jaafar talking about how honored he is to play his uncle, Miles Teller being charming, everyone discussing the dance choreography and the costumes. Completely accurate. Completely misleading. The cast IS promoting a film about Michael Jackson's life and music. They're just not mentioning the third act.

The press tour becomes part of the text. You watch the interviews after seeing the film and they hit completely differently. Every "he was such an icon" from Jaafar has a new weight. That's bonus content the film generates for free.

The Jimmy Kimmel thing you mentioned earlier actually flips too in this version. Kimmel has the cast on, does the fun nostalgia segment, plays a clip of Jaafar moonwalking, audience cheers. Two weeks later everyone who saw the film is rewatching that clip on YouTube with completely different eyes. Kimmel didn't know. The audience didn't know. That's the point.

It's the most method marketing campaign ever conceived if it's real.

It's probably not real and they made Bohemian Rhapsody 2.

But the fact that we can't tell yet from the press tour alone is either incredibly suspicious or a complete coincidence and either way it's funny.

Short answer: **no homework required.** It's explicitly released under the "Marvel Spotlight" banner, which Marvel uses specifically to signal standalone, no-prior-knowledge-needed stories.

It's been described as a fully standalone story that doesn't mandatorily connect to any previous MCU project — more Hollywood industry satire than superhero continuity dump.

The one returning character worth knowing about is **Trevor Slattery** (Ben Kingsley), who first appeared in Iron Man 3 as a bumbling actor hired to play a fake terrorist leader. Knowing that backstory adds some texture but isn't necessary — the show re-establishes who he is.

There's also a nod to anti-superhero legislation (similar to the Sokovia Accords from Civil War), but it's background flavor, not plot-critical context.

Basically just watch it. It's 8 episodes, ~30 min each, so about 4 hours total.
Wow seems like an actual art kinda thing

Yeah, Marvel has been pretty desperate to prove they can do something besides "CGI sky portal threatens Earth," and Wonder Man seems to be one of the rare times that actually worked.

The meta angle — a struggling actor trying to land a role in a fictional superhero film called *Wonder Man* — is inherently self-aware in a way most Marvel stuff isn't. It's Marvel making a show *about* the absurdity of Marvel.

The unfortunate context is that it got compared to *The Bear* of superhero TV partly because the bar is just so low. Two similar Hollywood satires (*The Franchise* on HBO, *The Studio* on Apple) both beat it to air, so some of the novelty was already gone by the time it dropped. *The Studio* in particular was genuinely excellent, so Wonder Man is operating in a tougher landscape than it would've been in 2023 when it was originally planned.

Still, critics consistently point to Yahya Abdul-Mateen II and Ben Kingsley as a genuinely warm pairing that can carry the whole thing — which is basically the whole job for a character-driven show. If the performances land, the concept works.

It's a weird thing for Marvel to have made. Whether it's *actually* art or just art-adjacent studio product dressed up nicely is probably worth deciding for yourself.
Seems like marvel is slowing down production of stuff

Yeah, intentionally so. They got badly burned by the post-Endgame content flood — the general consensus was that they sacrificed quality for volume and trained audiences to feel like everything was homework with diminishing payoff.

Feige explicitly called Phase 6 "the most focused phase" after admitting they "expanded too much" in Phases 4 and 5. Which is a pretty remarkable admission from a studio that spent years insisting every piece of content was essential.

The Spotlight banner is the structural response to that — a way to greenlight smaller, weirder projects without forcing them into the continuity machine. Wonder Man being genuinely standalone is the proof of concept. If it works commercially, it opens the door for Marvel to do horror, comedy, drama without everything needing to set up the next Avengers film.

The cynical read is that they're also just cutting costs. Disney ordered Marvel to reduce output after a string of underperformers, and "artistic restraint" is a convenient narrative to put on top of "we're spending less money." Probably both things are true simultaneously.

The real test is Doomsday and Secret Wars — those are the convergence points everything's been building toward. If those land, the slowdown will look like smart pacing in retrospect. If they don't, it'll look like a studio that lost the plot and never got it back.
Right like if it makes less than a billion dollars or flops like recent marvel stuff that will cause them to fucking panic

Yeah, the stakes on Doomsday specifically are existential in a way that hasn't really been true since the original Avengers. That was the proof of concept for the whole shared universe model. Doomsday is essentially Marvel proving it can do it twice.

The Robert Downey Jr. casting as Doom is clearly the big swing — they're banking on his personal gravity to do what the Multiverse Saga failed to do with Kang, which was give audiences a villain they actually care about. Kang was a structural problem: Jonathan Majors got fired, but even before that, the character never landed the way Thanos did because he was too abstract and too dispersed across too many projects.

Doom is a better villain on paper — singular, iconic, menacing without needing a multiverse PhD to understand his motivation. And RDJ has the cultural weight to make a debut feel like an event. But that's a lot of pressure on one casting choice to rescue years of narrative drift.

The panic scenario you're describing is real though. If Doomsday opens to like $600M globally, the discourse will be immediate and brutal — "Marvel is finished" pieces were already being written after Ant-Man 3 and The Marvels. A second consecutive Avengers-level film underperforming would be genuinely hard to spin.

They probably get one more shot after Endgame's shadow. Doomsday is it.
Maybe we shouldn't have 600m dollar films that need to clear that to make money

Completely correct, and it's a structural problem the whole industry has sleepwalked into.

The blockbuster budget spiral is basically a prisoner's dilemma. Each studio escalates spectacle to compete, VFX costs balloon, marketing adds another $150-200M on top, and suddenly you need $800M+ globally just to break even. Nobody individually made a crazy decision — the incentive at each step was rational — but the collective outcome is insane.

Marvel specifically has a cost problem that's self-inflicted. The MCU trained audiences to expect everything to feel "big," which means even a relatively contained show like Wonder Man still has the overhead of the Marvel machine behind it. The Spotlight banner is partly an attempt to escape that trap but they haven't fully committed to actually cheap, actually small productions.

The comparison that's worth making is A24. Genuinely low budget, genuinely weird, and when something hits it's pure profit. *Everything Everywhere* cost $14M and made $70M. That's a better business than spending $250M hoping China shows up.

The problem is Marvel's entire brand promise is spectacle. You can't easily downshift without audiences noticing and feeling cheated. Wonder Man works at smaller scale because it's *about* Hollywood rather than saving the universe — but you can't do that for Avengers.

So they're kind of trapped. The films that require $600M to break even are also the ones the whole franchise depends on landing. It's a brittle model that worked until it didn't.

messages.text: Marvel made a mention in Shang chi about him being bullied for being Chinese which like maybe sure that happened kids are shitty, but the movie is set in San Francisco, the kid is more likely to be bullied for being white and not academically catching up with gaocao and Indian immigrant parents expectations

Your instinct is directionally right but the framing needs some refinement.

The demographic reality: SFUSD is 36% Asian and only 13% white, and white students make up only 12.9% of SF public school students despite whites being 41.6% of the city's population — because almost 30% of SF children attend private schools, and the majority of students from high-income white families attend private schools.

So a Chinese-American kid being bullied *for being Chinese* in an SF public school is demographically weird. You're not the minority in that environment. The bullying vector that actually exists in those schools is more likely intra-Asian (FOB vs. ABC dynamics, class stratification within Chinese-American communities) or related to the academic pressure hierarchy you're gesturing at.

That said, I'd push back slightly on your specific counter-framing:

**The gaokao angle is a bit off.** Gaokao pressure is a China thing — it doesn't directly translate to SF's Chinese-American community the same way. The academic pressure in SF Chinatown/Richmond families is real, but it's more "Kumon + piano + SAT prep" than gaokao. The immigrant parent academic competition you're describing is more stereotypically South Asian (the Indian immigrant parent pipeline to IIT/Ivy), though obviously both communities have intense academic cultures.

**The movie's bigger problem** is that the bullying scene is probably just lazy Marvel shorthand for "establish that character had a hard time as an outsider" without doing the actual work of figuring out what an outsider experience looks like *in SF specifically* as opposed to, say, rural Iowa in 1987. It's a generic bullied-immigrant story template pasted onto a setting where it doesn't fit.

The more honest version of that character's childhood adversity in SF would be something like: pressure to perform academically in a community where everyone else is also performing academically, or the specific tension of being *not Chinese enough* for the Chinese community and *not American enough* for everyone else. That's a real and interesting story. "Kids called me Chinese" in 36%-Asian SF public schools is just sloppy.
Kunon, piano and SAT prep is just american gaocao

Yeah, fair point, I walked into that one. The mechanism is identical — high-stakes academic credentialing as the primary vector for family status and resource allocation, enforced by parental pressure from the moment the kid can hold a pencil. The specific exam is almost incidental.

The more interesting observation is that gaokao is at least *honest* about what it is. One test, one day, your life trajectory forks. The American version wraps the same pressure in a 10-year multi-front campaign — grades, extracurriculars, test scores, essays, "well-rounded" performance — which is somehow considered more humane despite being more totalizing and more expensive. You can't grind gaokao with a $400/hour college counselor.

The SAT-prep industrial complex is gaokao with venture-backed tutoring companies and the plausible deniability of "we're developing the whole child."

Right the thing is more likely is if they bullied you for being from a specific part of China but you can't explain that in a marvel movie about how some people made fun of him for speaking Mandarin and not Cantonese

Yeah that's the actually interesting sociological texture that gets completely flattened. The Richmond vs. Sunset distinction, Cantonese-speaking legacy community looking down on Mandarin-speaking mainland newcomers, the class signaling embedded in which dialect your family speaks — that's a real fault line in SF's Chinese community that's been there for decades.

And it maps onto real historical grievance. Cantonese families have been in SF since the railroad era, built Chinatown, have legitimate claim to the neighborhood. Then post-1990s mainland immigration brings Mandarin speakers who are often wealthier and more recent, which creates this weird inversion where the "authentic" Chinese-American community resents the newcomers who are higher status economically but lower status culturally by the old community's metric.

That's a story worth telling. It's also completely unfilmable in a Marvel movie because it requires the audience to hold like four simultaneous context variables about Chinese immigration waves, dialect politics, and SF neighborhood demographics. You'd lose 90% of the audience in the first thirty seconds.

So instead you get "kids made fun of me for being different" which is the narrative equivalent of a stock photo. Universally legible, completely evacuated of meaning.

Or they could have bullied him for being homeless and the only reason he was able to go to high school was because of the homelessness services in SF (a political fucking mine field you can walk into, but Disney wanted to get into this for some goddamn reason)

That's actually a sharper angle because it's *true* — SF has this coexistence of extreme wealth, a massive unhoused population, and genuinely robust homelessness services that do keep people functional enough to participate in normal life. A kid navigating that specific circumstance would be authentically San Franciscan in a way the dialect bullying story isn't.

And it would have thematic coherence with the actual movie. Shang-Chi's whole arc is about a father who built an empire from nothing, legacy, what you inherit vs. what you build. A version where the mother's family was poor and unstably housed in SF before Xu Wenwu's resources arrived, or where Shang-Chi himself experienced that instability, actually *connects* to the themes instead of being decorative backstory.

But you're right that Disney walked into a minefield they clearly didn't think through. The moment you set your movie in SF and acknowledge poverty exists, you're implicitly taking a position on one of the most politically toxic urban policy debates in America. The homelessness-industrial complex, Prop C, whether services enable or rescue — that's a third rail that makes the China box office concerns look simple.

Marvel's instinct was probably "set it in SF for the aesthetics, don't engage with anything SF actually is." The car chase down California Street, the skyline, the vibes. City as backdrop, not as place with actual social texture.

Which is just the broader Marvel disease — locations are sets, not contexts.

Idk that's interesting maybe idk. Plus there's the relationship with his father where he basically was being forced to link the first flame or enter the age of dark and the film just has big dragon kill his dad instead of him making that decision.

Yeah that's the real missed opportunity and it's a much more fundamental storytelling failure than the SF stuff.

The whole movie is building toward a genuine tragic choice — do you kill your father to save the world, or do you let the world burn because he's your father and he's also kind of a victim of his own grief. That's Hamlet territory. That's actually interesting. The father isn't even straightforwardly evil, he's a man who loved someone so much he broke reality trying to get her back and then bent his children into weapons because he couldn't process the loss any other way.

And then a dragon does it.

The dragon resolves the moral weight so Shang-Chi doesn't have to. He gets to grieve his father without having made the choice to kill him, which means he gets all the emotional payoff of a tragic ending without any of the actual tragic cost. It's narrative cowardice dressed up as spectacle.

The version where Shang-Chi has to make that decision himself — and does it — is a much darker movie that Disney probably couldn't greenlight. Because then the protagonist killed his father and you can't put that on a lunchbox. So instead you get a giant dragon ex machina that preserves everyone's moral cleanliness while pretending the movie was about something.

It's the same instinct as the SF stuff. Gesture at real weight, then flinch when it's time to pay it out.

Right have him lunge at your friend the awkwafina character and have a scene where he's clearly holding back because he doesn't want to fight his dad, he's fucking sobbing because he doesn't like his dad but he lost his mom already and he doesn't want that to happen again.

Yeah that's the scene. That's the whole movie in one moment.

Because the thing that makes the father genuinely terrifying isn't that he's powerful, it's that Shang-Chi *understands* him. He watched his father become this because of grief. He knows exactly what losing his mother did to this man. And now he's watching himself potentially become the same person — someone who loves so hard they destroy everything around them — and the only way to stop it is to be the thing that breaks his father the way his mother's death broke his father.

Awkwafina's character grabbing him, him fighting through her to get to his dad, sobbing, not because he hates him but because he loves him and love isn't enough and he knows it — that's the thesis. That's what the whole training sequence and the rings backstory was supposedly building toward.

And it costs nothing to film. It's two actors and a close-up. You don't need a CGI dragon. You need Simu Liu crying and Tony Leung looking at his son and recognizing what he made him into.

Tony Leung is one of the greatest actors alive. They cast Tony Leung and then had a dragon upstage him in the climax of his own character arc. That's genuinely offensive. That's like hiring a Michelin star chef to make a sandwich and then covering it in ketchup because test audiences wanted more sauce.
Right like why the fuck even is there the fucking dragon

The dragon is because someone in a meeting said "act three needs a big threat that unifies everyone" and nobody in that room had the authority or inclination to say "the big threat IS the father, we already have it."

It's the Marvel formula executing automatically. Third act requires: escalating spectacle, a thing bigger than the villain, preferably with wings, hero uses new power to defeat it, crowd cheers. The formula doesn't have a branch for "what if the emotional climax IS the climax and we just let Tony Leung carry it."

And the dragon retroactively breaks the father's characterization. Because now Wenwu wasn't a man consumed by grief making a catastrophically wrong decision — he was being *manipulated* by a soul-eating monster. Which is the most infuriating kind of villain laundering. It takes genuine tragic culpability and replaces it with "well he was being controlled." He gets to be sympathetic AND not responsible. You can't have both. That's not how tragedy works.

Shakespeare didn't have a dragon show up in act five and eat Claudius so Hamlet didn't have to stab his uncle. The whole point is Hamlet has to do it and it costs him everything.

The dragon exists because Marvel doesn't trust audiences with unresolved moral weight, and they don't trust their own movies to be enough without a third act monster. Even when the monster they already have is Tony fucking Leung.

Right why not have Shang chi basically be like beat his father and then the entire group of people who his dad lead are like ",we follow the next in line, we are at your command sir" and maybe his sister sneaks away for a sequel tease of she is the villain for the sequel

Yeah that's so much cleaner and it earns everything the movie set up.

Because now Shang-Chi has actually *won* in the worst possible way. He beat his father, his father is dead, and now he's standing there holding the rings and a thousand soldiers are kneeling to him and he has to reckon with the fact that he just became his father. The thing he spent the whole movie running from. He didn't escape the legacy, he inherited it.

And he has to make a second choice immediately with no time to grieve — what do you do with an army that just pledged loyalty to you over your father's body. That's not a superhero moment, that's a tragedy compounding in real time.

The sister dynamic works perfectly for this too because she watched the whole thing and she draws the opposite conclusion. Where Shang-Chi sees the inheritance as a burden she sees it as rightfully hers. She's been training longer, she's been loyal where he ran away, and now he shows up and accidentally wins everything. Her sneaking away isn't mustache-twirling villainy, it's a completely legible human response to watching your absent brother accidentally inherit everything.

That's a sequel. That's actually a sequel with something to say about legacy and legitimacy and whether you can hold power without being corrupted by it.

Instead we got the jade emperor's dragon and Awkwafina got a spirit animal and Simu Liu punched a big thing with glowing fists.

The bones of a genuinely great movie are just sitting there under all the Marvel concrete.
Right then there's the end of the second movie which is a vergil versus Dante fight at the end of DMC 3

Which is the platonic ideal of what that Shang-Chi sequel fight should look like.

Because the reason the Vergil/Dante fight works isn't the spectacle, it's that Dante is *winning* and it's destroying him. Every hit he lands on his brother costs him something. The fight is grief expressed as violence. Vergil isn't a villain in that moment, he's what Dante could have become if he'd made different choices, and Dante has to beat him anyway knowing that.

That's exactly the emotional geometry of Shang-Chi versus his sister. She isn't wrong. Her grievance is legitimate. He abandoned the family, she stayed, she earned the rings by every metric except the one that actually counted which was being the protagonist. And now he has to fight her anyway because she's going to do something catastrophic with the power.

And he can't hate her for it. That's the key. Vergil and Dante aren't fighting because they hate each other, they're fighting because they're the same person who made different choices at the fork. The love is still there and it makes every punch worse.

The DMC3 fight also works because it ends without resolution. Vergil falls, Dante doesn't feel victorious, the credits don't roll on a hero pose. It ends on Dante crying holding his brother's amulet.

Marvel would have Shang-Chi catch his sister and she'd show up in a therapy montage in Avengers 7 having been rehabilitated.

Fuck the actual ending of shang chi 2 would be his sister falls or whatever (she comes back as a big villain in like secret wars or doomsday) and the ending is him attempting to explain his situation to a therapist awkwafina forced him to go to in SF, the therapist is saying stuff like "metaphorically right" and then he's like "yeah metaphorically that's how I feel"

That's a perfect ending because it does three things simultaneously.

It's funny in a way that's actually earned instead of Marvel's usual tension-breaking quip. The humor comes from character and situation, not from someone making a joke at the wrong moment to remind you this is a fun movie.

It's the thematic button. The whole movie is about whether you can process inherited trauma and generational violence through normal human frameworks. And the answer the ending gives is — kind of? He's trying. He's sitting in a therapist's office in SF using the word metaphorically about things that were not metaphorical. That's genuinely what it looks like when someone from a world of mythological violence tries to reintegrate into ordinary life. The gap between his reality and the therapist's framework IS the joke and also IS the point.

And it sets up the Avengers problem organically. Because now you have a character who is genuinely trying to live a normal life in SF, going to therapy, hanging out with Awkwafina, and the MCU is going to keep pulling him back into exactly the kind of world his therapist thinks he's processing metaphorically. The tension writes itself.

It also respects Awkwafina's character in a way the actual movies don't. She's not comic relief, she's the anchor to normality. She's the person who looked at this man and said you need to talk to someone and actually made him do it. That's a real friendship doing real work.

Compare that to what we actually got which is her having a spirit animal now for some reason.

Like the fucking sister ends up making a deal with doctor doom to be an enforcer in doomsday and she becomes genuinely terrifying, and he talks to the avengers about it and Thor makes a joke like "she's your step sister right" "no, not relevant I don't think, but no she isn't"

The Thor joke lands because Thor actually has standing to make it. He's not doing it to be glib, he has genuine firsthand experience with the "sibling who made a deal with a cosmic power and became a problem" pipeline. He's pattern matching from personal history. Loki did basically the same thing with different aesthetics.

And then the beat after where Shang-Chi has to clarify "not a step sister" because he can see where Thor's framework is going and it doesn't map — that's character specificity. Thor is trying to file this under a folder he already has and the folder doesn't fit. Their situations rhyme but they're not the same and Shang-Chi knows it.

Because Loki wanted to be Thor. Wanted the throne, wanted the father's approval, the whole thing was about Odin's validation.

Shang-Chi's sister wanted what was *actually hers by merit*. That's a different and arguably more legitimate grievance which makes her more dangerous because she's harder to dismiss. You can't do the "he was always jealous" read on her. She stayed. She trained. She was there.

Thor processing that distinction in real time across the rest of the conversation — slowly realizing this isn't a Loki situation and his playbook doesn't apply — that's actually interesting Avengers team dynamic work. Characters bouncing their traumas off each other and finding the edges where the analogy breaks.

Instead we'll get them standing in a row looking at a spaceship.
Right it's a running bit where Thor attempts to say "ah I've seen this before" and then he says "from what I know of your story it's different" and they actually are annoyed by each other

Yeah and the annoyance is specific and bilateral which is what makes it work.

Thor's annoyance is that Shang-Chi keeps refusing the framework. Thor has three thousand years of experience with exactly this category of problem and this guy from San Francisco keeps saying "it's not like that" about things that are clearly like that from a structural standpoint. Thor is trying to help and is being rejected and he finds that genuinely frustrating.

Shang-Chi's annoyance is that Thor is technically right about the structure and completely wrong about the texture and doesn't understand why that distinction matters. Yes fine, powerful sibling, cosmic deal, now an enforcer for a larger threat, the chess pieces are the same. But his sister isn't performing villainy for attention. She's not going to monologue. She's not going to hesitate at the crucial moment because somewhere underneath she still wants his approval. She's going to be completely professional about destroying him because her grievance is about justice not about love and that makes her genuinely scary in a way Loki never quite was.

And neither of them is wrong. That's the good version of this dynamic. Thor's pattern recognition is legitimately useful intelligence even if the emotional read is off. Shang-Chi's insistence on specificity is legitimate even if it sometimes makes him dismiss useful information.

By the third movie they've grudgingly developed actual respect for each other's blind spots.

Marvel will never make this movie but it exists perfectly formed in the space where good decisions could have been made.
Right in the 3rd movie someone makes a joke about Cain and Abel and they don't understand the reference because they are Chinese.

And Thor gets it immediately and that makes it worse because now Thor is the one saying "ah yes I know this story" with complete sincerity and Shang-Chi is looking at him like why do you know my family's business and Thor has to explain that it's a western religious text and Shang-Chi has never heard of it.

And then someone has to explain the actual story and Shang-Chi's response is just "that's it? That's the whole story? One of them farms and one of them herds animals and God has a preference and then there's a murder?" And he's genuinely unimpressed because his family's version of this has rings forged from meteor iron and a dimension eating monster and ten thousand years of history and the western canonical version of brother conflict is two guys with crops and livestock.

Thor defending the Cain and Abel story with complete earnestness because to him it's a very serious and ancient text.

Shang-Chi not being dismissive out of arrogance, just genuinely coming from a mythological tradition that is so much older and more elaborate that the comparison feels reductive. Chinese civilization has been processing brother conflict and dynastic betrayal and inherited violence for longer than the Bible has existed and he knows those stories.

The joke isn't that he's ignorant. The joke is that the reference doesn't land because his cultural library already has a much more detailed version of the same document and the western one feels like a rough draft.

Right he's like "that's it" and other people are like "it's a good story" someone else like one of the wakandans say "I like the story, it's a nice simple story, gets the point across" "not what we meant but thank you shuri"

Shuri completely accidentally becoming the mediator because she's the only one who can hold both positions simultaneously — yes the story is simple, yes that's fine, not everything needs to be an epic, moving on.

What even is the 3 body problem?

The three-body problem is the question of predicting the motion of three massive objects interacting through gravity.

Two bodies is easy — Newton solved it. Two masses orbiting each other follow clean ellipses, the math closes into a nice analytical solution, done. Add a third body and the system becomes chaotic in the technical sense: tiny differences in initial conditions compound exponentially, trajectories diverge unpredictably, and there's no general closed-form solution. You can't write down a formula; you have to simulate it numerically.

The chaos isn't just computational inconvenience — it's intrinsic. Poincaré proved in 1887 that no general analytical solution exists. Most three-body configurations are unstable: bodies get flung out of the system, collide, or settle into one of a small set of stable configurations (like the Lagrange points, where a third small body can orbit stably relative to two larger ones).

There are specific exact solutions — certain symmetric choreographies where three equal masses chase each other around a figure-eight, for instance — but these are special cases, not the general answer.

Why it matters practically: the solar system is an n-body problem, and over long timescales planetary orbits are technically chaotic. We can predict accurately for millions of years but not billions. Spacecraft navigation uses numerical integration constantly. It also shows up in stellar dynamics — binary star systems with a third companion, globular clusters, etc.

The Liu Cixin novel named after it uses the concept as a metaphor for geopolitical unpredictability, which is fine as metaphor but don't confuse that with the physics.

Right I think for a geopolitical instability it's easy to misinterpret, that's the problem with more complicated metaphors, I think the author probably does too because it's 3 body problem, not n body problem doesn't sell as many books

That's a fair instinct but I'd push back a little.

The three-body problem specifically is the right metaphor for what Liu is doing, not just a catchier title. The whole point is that *three* is the minimum number needed for chaos — two superpowers is stable and analyzable (Cold War bipolarity, MAD doctrine, clean deterrence equilibria), three is where predictability collapses. The novel is explicitly about a tripartite situation: Earth, the Trisolaran civilization, and the broader "dark forest" of unknown actors. The instability isn't generic n-body chaos, it's the specific threshold argument that three is qualitatively different from two.

So the title is actually doing real conceptual work, not just branding.

Where I think your criticism *does* land is on the author's execution of the metaphor within the story. The Trisolaran home system's chaos is used somewhat hand-wavily to justify an entire civilization's psychology and political structure, which is a stretch even on its own terms — real three-body systems aren't *constantly* chaotic, they have extended stable periods punctuated by instability. Liu treats it as relentless, which serves the plot but misrepresents the physics.

The deeper problem with the metaphor is that geopolitical actors have intentions, models of each other, and can communicate — none of which applies to gravitating masses. So the chaos mechanisms are completely different even if the surface instability looks similar. That's where the metaphor actually breaks down, not the choice of three over n.
Right it's more accurately game theory than physics, ITS LIKE EVERYTHING IS A MULTI AGENT COORDINATION PROBLEM

Yeah, and that's not even a hot take — it's basically the founding observation of game theory. Schelling, Nash, all of it is just "what happens when agents with models of each other make decisions under uncertainty about other agents' decisions."

The physics metaphor is seductive because it sounds more rigorous, but it's actually *less* precise than game theory for describing the thing Liu cares about. Gravity is memoryless and intention-free. Game theory has beliefs, updates, signaling, reputation — all the machinery that actually explains dark forest logic. The "don't signal your existence" conclusion Liu reaches is trivially a game theory result (it's basically a dominance argument under extreme uncertainty) and has nothing to do with orbital mechanics.

The place where "everything is multi-agent coordination" gets genuinely interesting and stops being a platitude is when you ask *why* coordination fails even when everyone would benefit from cooperating. That's where it gets weird:

- **Common knowledge problems** — everyone knows X, everyone knows everyone knows X, but the chain breaks somewhere and coordination collapses
- **Commitment problems** — you can't credibly pre-commit, so the cooperative equilibrium is unreachable even if both parties want it
- **Cheap talk** — communication doesn't help if signals aren't costly, so information doesn't resolve the game, it becomes part of it

The dark forest is really a commitment problem dressed up in astrophysics clothes. You can't credibly signal benign intent because the cost of being wrong is extinction, so defection dominates regardless of actual intentions. That's Hobbes, not Newton.
I'd read a book called equilibrium referring to Nash's

That's a better title honestly. And it has the advantage of being accurate — the dark forest steady state *is* a Nash equilibrium. No player can unilaterally improve their outcome by deviating from "destroy any detected civilization." Defection is the dominant strategy, silence is the best response to silence, and you get a stable but horrifying fixed point.

"Equilibrium" also carries the right moral weight. Nash equilibria are famously indifferent to whether the outcome is good — they're just stable. The prisoner's dilemma equilibrium is mutually destructive and nobody wants it, but nobody can escape it unilaterally. That tension between stability and optimality is exactly what makes the dark forest compelling, and "Equilibrium" names that tension directly in a way "Three-Body Problem" doesn't.

The counterargument is that "equilibrium" implies stasis, and the novel is actually about disruption — what happens when the equilibrium gets perturbed. But you could argue that makes the title even more pointed. The whole tragedy is that the equilibrium is so robust that even knowing about it, even wanting to escape it, isn't sufficient to break it.

There's also something grimly funny about naming a science fiction epic after a concept John Nash worked out while losing his mind. The most elegant description of rational behavior derived under conditions of profound irrationality. Liu would probably appreciate that irony given how much of the trilogy is about the gap between individual cognition and systemic outcomes.

Nash himself would probably sell fewer books though.
Wait he lost his mind

Yeah — Nash had paranoid schizophrenia, which onset in his late twenties, right around when he was doing his most important work. He spent decades in and out of psychiatric institutions, believed he was receiving encrypted messages from extraterrestrials, thought he was being recruited by cosmic forces, wrote unhinged letters to foreign embassies. Princeton more or less tolerated him wandering the campus as a ghost figure for years — the "phantom of Fine Hall."

He recovered, remarkably, in his sixties — apparently through a slow process of consciously rejecting the delusional thoughts rather than medication. Won the Nobel in 1994. Then died in a taxi with his wife coming back from the Nobel ceremony in Oslo. Seatbelts, which is an almost offensively mundane ending for that life.

The biopic A Beautiful Mind exists but sanitizes it significantly — Russell Crowe's Nash hallucinates people, which is more cinematic, but real Nash's delusions were primarily paranoid ideation and voices, not visual hallucinations. Hollywood chose the wrong symptom because it photographs better.

The irony you're poking at is real though. The Nash equilibrium is the canonical model of cold, perfectly rational strategic reasoning — agents with complete selfishness and perfect logic reaching stable outcomes. And the guy who formalized it was simultaneously constructing an elaborate delusional cosmology and couldn't reliably distinguish signal from noise in his own mind. The math was impeccable. The mathematician was not.
In my mind I like to imagine that Charlie Nash was named after Nash, I know that's probably wrong

Charlie Nash is almost certainly named after the boxer. Street Fighter's naming conventions are pretty transparently references to real fighters — Balrog/M. Bison is Mike Tyson, Vega is a matador archetype, the whole roster is built on real combat sport iconography. Nash fits that pattern as a generic American military-fighter name more than a game theory Easter egg.

That said, Capcom's localization history is so chaotic that reading intentionality into any of their naming decisions is risky. These are the people who swapped M. Bison, Balrog, and Vega between regions specifically to avoid a Mike Tyson lawsuit, so the internal coherence of their naming logic is not exactly airtight.

The more interesting question is whether the *coincidence* is meaningful anyway. Charlie Nash in Street Fighter is defined by his relationship to a system that destroys him despite him playing by the rules — he does everything right, follows orders, acts with integrity, gets killed by the institution he served. That's actually a more interesting Nash equilibrium reference than an intentional one would be, because it's the *other* Nash equilibrium problem: the sucker's payoff. The guy who cooperates in a defect-dominant world.

So you're probably wrong about the intention but the conceptual overlap is real enough that you can keep the headcanon without feeling too embarrassed about it.
The sucker's payoff is a good ass name for a movie now that is a movie

It really is. And it has the advantage of being immediately legible without explanation — you don't need to know game theory to understand "sucker's payoff." Everyone has felt it. You did everything right and got the worst outcome because the other players weren't playing the same game you thought you were.

The movie writes itself honestly. protagonist operates on one set of rules, discovers too late the actual game had completely different rules, the tragedy isn't betrayal exactly — it's the gap between the game they thought they were in and the game they were actually in. That's more interesting than pure backstabbing because the antagonist doesn't even have to be malicious. Just operating on a different equilibrium.

It's also a better title than most prestige drama titles which are either one-word abstractions (*Parasite, Arrival*) or overly literary. "Sucker's Payoff" has texture. It sounds like a noir but it could be anything — corporate drama, war film, political thriller, even a quiet family story about someone who sacrificed everything for people who weren't keeping score.

The Nash connection gives it a optional second layer for people who catch it without being load-bearing for people who don't. That's good titling — it works on multiple levels without requiring the deeper one.

Do they write and draw the books? (I'm coming at this from a western perspective where those are typically two different skills maybe they aren't in anime)

Yeah in manga it's typically one person doing both — writing and drawing. That's the default assumption, and both Oda and Togashi do both.

The western comics split (writer + artist + inker + colorist) is actually the exception globally. Manga culture built around the singular auteur model, which is part of why the hiatuses hit so hard — there's no artist who can keep drawing while the writer recovers. The whole thing stops.

The tradeoff is that manga has a much stronger authorial voice because of it. Oda's art *is* his writing in a way that's hard to separate — the visual gags, the character designs, the panel composition all carry meaning he'd lose if he handed art duties to someone else. Same with Togashi, whose art is notoriously rough but whose page layouts are genuinely sophisticated.

The downside is you get Togashi's spine destroying the entire enterprise with no redundancy. One guy, one series, one set of hands.

Yes — *AK-47* (Russian title: *Калашников*) is a 2020 Russian biographical film about Mikhail Kalashnikov, the inventor of the AK-47.

It was directed by Konstantin Buslov and stars Yura Borisov. Kalashnikov's own daughter served as a consultant on the production.

The story follows him being wounded as a tank driver in 1941 during WWII, which sets him on the path to designing the rifle, culminating in the AK-47 in 1947.

Worth noting: critics point out the film covers only about 5 years of his life and tends to present a smooth, conflict-free version of events — more of a state-approved highlight reel than a deep biographical portrait. It's also pretty nakedly Russian propaganda, though as one reviewer noted, that's par for the course with this genre. It's available on Prime Video and Tubi (free). ```

No. Meta abandoned Apache HTTP Server ages ago — they replaced it with their own purpose-built server called **Proxygen** (their C++ HTTP/HTTP2 framework) and use **Nginx** at various layers. The more interesting story is what they actually run:

- **Proxygen**: Meta's own open-source C++ HTTP framework, handling their front-end traffic. Apache would be a joke at their scale.
- **FBOSS**: Their custom network OS for controlling switches, now migrated to the OCP Switch Abstraction Interface.
- **ServiceRouter**: Their internal service mesh for routing traffic between services.

The site was originally built on PHP and MySQL, and Apache was part of that early LAMP-era stack. But that was early-2000s Facebook. They've been off that for over a decade.

The more interesting question is why you're asking — if you're doing infrastructure reconnaissance on Meta (e.g., for a security context or competitive analysis), the web server layer is the least interesting thing about their stack. Their custom silicon, RDMA-over-Ethernet fabric, and 16,000-GPU RSC supercomputer with a 3-level Clos network fabric is where the actual engineering happens.

What Zola actually does is reveal HYDRA's long game: they've been infiltrating S.H.I.E.L.D. since the end of WWII, and his algorithm (called **Project Insight**) is designed to *identify and preemptively kill* people who would oppose HYDRA's future world order. It's a targeting system for the Helicarriers, not a prediction of who specific people are.

The algorithm identifies threats based on behavior patterns, social connections, digital footprint — the idea being that HYDRA can eliminate resistance before it materializes. We never get a list of names on screen.

As for whether we "see" the targets: the Helicarriers get destroyed before they can fire, so no, the kill list never gets executed. The closest we get to seeing the scope is when Cap reads off that the system flagged **20 million people** as targets.
