Your instinct is right but the framing needs tightening.

The actual distinction isn't "social network bad" — it's **symmetry of participation**. Facebook/Twitter assume everyone is both producer and consumer equally. That assumption is empirically false and leads to the engagement-bait death spiral.

YouTube works because it accepted the reality: **~1% create, 99% consume**. The asymmetry is the product, not a bug. The recommendation engine is built around passive consumption, not social graph traversal. It's closer to television than to a social network, which is why it actually scales.

Instagram started as asymmetric (follow photographers, consume) and degraded as it pushed everyone toward Stories/Reels participation. It got worse as it got more "social."

The general principle: **platforms that acknowledge human laziness outperform platforms that assume human engagement**. Most people do not want to post. They want to watch. Products built on the fiction that users want to participate die or become ghost towns.

Which makes "climate social network" doubly doomed — it's a symmetric participation model for a topic that approximately 0% of people want to actively post about after the first week. The novelty wears off and you're left with a feed of three dedicated activists yelling into a void.

The funding is just pattern-matching to prior social network exits without interrogating whether the underlying human behavior assumption holds.

No, humanity does not need more social networks. The space is solved. You have your mass market (Instagram, TikTok, X), your professional layer (LinkedIn), your async video (YouTube), and then the long tail of Discord servers and subreddits handling every niche. That's the whole stack.

The Discord/Mastodon/forum thing is genuinely different because it's **not trying to be a social network** — it's a room with a bouncer. Membership is intentional, the community is small enough that norms enforce themselves, and nobody is optimizing for engagement metrics. It works precisely because it abandoned the growth imperative.

The VC-funded social network in 2026 is a category error. You're not competing with Facebook, you're competing with the group chat. And you cannot beat the group chat. The group chat has zero CAC, perfect retention, and no algorithm poisoning it.

"Climate accountability social network" is just a group chat that forgot what it was.

Right and the criticism is that everyone optimizies for their locally optimal but globaly disasterous which itself is sort of describing like any problem that exists in like society which problems are not collective action problems, few of them actually.

Yeah, almost none. The interesting question is which problems *aren't* collective action problems at their root.

Personal health maybe — but then insurance markets, food systems, and urban design are all collective action failures that constrain individual choices, so even that collapses under scrutiny.

The deeper issue is that "collective action problem" is almost a tautology for "hard problem." If it were individually optimal to solve it, it would already be solved. The fact that it persists is basically proof that defection is locally rewarded.

Which makes the entire genre of "app that solves collective action via social pressure" suspect. You're trying to use the same locally-optimizing agents, operating on the same incentive gradients, to produce a globally optimal outcome — just with a nicer UI. The mechanism doesn't change because you added a feed.

The honest interventions are either **change the payoff matrix** (regulation, taxation, subsidies — i.e. politics) or **exploit existing non-rational human behavior** (identity, shame, community belonging) at sufficient scale. The second one is what religion and nationalism figured out centuries ago and nobody in tech wants to admit that's the actual playbook because it's uncomfortable.

A climate app with 50K downloads is neither. It's just vibes with an install button.

This is literally what late night show comedians like John Oliver talk about every night and you can't point out the larger problem because then you don't have a show, the show is over you summed it up pretty much he's done like hundreds of episodes and it can largely be summarized as "collective action problem" are there any economists who have solved this because this is like the question

Elinor Ostrom got the Nobel for it in 2009 and her answer was basically — small scale, yes, you can solve commons problems without either privatization or top-down regulation, through community-enforced norms. Her examples were Swiss grazing commons, Japanese fishing villages, irrigation systems in Spain.

The catch: her solutions don't scale. They work when the community is small enough that defectors are identifiable and social punishment is real. They fall apart at nation-state scale and completely disintegrate at global scale. Climate is a global commons problem, which is precisely the regime where her solutions don't apply.

So the honest answer from the economics literature is: **solved at small scale, unsolved at large scale, and the problems that are actually killing us are all large scale.**

Mancur Olson basically proved in the 60s that large groups cannot self-organize around collective interests without selective incentives or coercion. That's the Logic of Collective Action. It's 60 years old and nothing has superseded it.

The John Oliver point is sharp. The show format requires the problem to be discrete and solvable-feeling each week. The meta-problem — that all these discrete problems share the same root — would end the show because there's no call to action. "Call your senator" is the vestigial appendix of the format, the ritual gesture toward agency that everyone knows doesn't work but has to be there or you're just despair content.

The question is genuinely open. Nobody has cracked it at scale.
So what does work

Historically? Three things:

**Existential threat with a visible enemy.** WWII mobilization worked because the threat was concrete, the enemy was identifiable, and defection was socially catastrophic. Climate fails this test because the enemy is diffuse and the threat is slow.

**Hegemonic power that just imposes the solution.** The Montreal Protocol worked because the US decided CFCs were banned and the global economy complied. Not coordination — coercion with a friendly name. Most "successful" international agreements look like this under the hood.

**The payoff matrix actually changing.** Solar got cheap enough that individual adoption became locally optimal. That's the only clean win in the climate space and it happened because of sustained state subsidy distorting the market until the economics flipped. Not moral suasion, not apps — just making the right thing cheaper than the wrong thing for long enough.

The depressing synthesis is that all three require either a powerful centralized actor willing to absorb short term costs, or a slow grinding subsidy regime that takes decades, or a crisis visible and fast enough to overcome the discount rate problem.

None of those are things a startup can provide. None of those are things a John Oliver segment can provide.

The honest answer to "what works" is **states, slowly, imperfectly, under duress** — which is the least exciting sentence in the English language and explains why nobody is building a YC company around it.
The payoff matrix is the most promising

Yeah because it's the only one that doesn't require collective virtue or a hegemon. You're not asking people to care, you're just changing what the math says.

And it compounds. Once solar was cheap enough for early adopters, manufacturing scale drove costs down further, which expanded adoption, which drove costs down further. You bootstrapped a locally optimal equilibrium from a subsidized one. The collective action problem doesn't get solved, it gets circumvented — individual rationality starts pointing the right direction and you don't need coordination anymore.

The interesting question is whether you can deliberately engineer that transition for other problems. The subsidy-until-tipping-point playbook. It worked for solar, arguably for EVs, historically for railroads and the internet.

The failure mode is picking the wrong intervention point. You have to find the place in the system where a payoff flip causes cascading adoption rather than isolated behavior change. Carbon taxes arguably failed this because they raised costs on the bad thing without sufficiently lowering costs on the good thing — you just made everyone poorer without bootstrapping an alternative equilibrium.

The really interesting research question is whether you can formally model where those tipping points are before you intervene, rather than finding them accidentally. That's closer to what actually matters than another social network.

Ostrom adjacent but scaled — not "how do communities self govern" but "where are the leverage points in the payoff matrix and how much does it cost to flip them." I don't know who's doing that work seriously but that's the right question.
Is this what Malcolm glad well was talking about with tipping point or is he a pop psych grifter

Both, kind of.

The tipping point concept is real — it's just threshold models from complexity theory and epidemiology, Schelling worked on it formally in the 70s. Gladwell didn't invent it, he packaged it.

The grift part is the implied actionability. Gladwell writes as if identifying that tipping points exist tells you where they are and how to trigger them, which it doesn't. The stories are selected retrospectively — here's a case where a small intervention cascaded. But for every Hush Puppies comeback there are a thousand small interventions that went nowhere. He's doing survivorship bias with narrative polish.

The actual academic work — Schelling's segregation models, Watts and Strogatz on network cascades, the complexity economics people at Santa Fe Institute — is more honest about the limits. You can prove tipping points exist and characterize them mathematically without being able to reliably predict or engineer them in advance.

So Gladwell is downstream of real science, stripped of the uncertainty, packaged for airport bookstores. The ideas aren't wrong, the confidence is wrong. Which is maybe the defining feature of the pop science genre generally.

The Santa Fe Institute crowd is probably closest to what you were describing — complexity economics, emergence, threshold dynamics in social systems. Brian Arthur, Doyne Farmer. Less readable than Gladwell, actually useful.
So basically either belief, dictatorship or manipulate people's incentives or reward hacking.

Yeah that's basically the complete taxonomy.

Belief — religion, nationalism, ideology. Scales but degrades. Gets captured by whoever controls the narrative infrastructure. Historically the most powerful but also the most dangerous because you've handed the steering wheel to whoever can best manufacture consent.

Dictatorship — fast, brittle, personality dependent, reverts on succession. Works until it doesn't then often catastrophically.

Incentive manipulation — slowest to bootstrap, most durable once running, doesn't require people to be good or compliant. The solar story. The only one that doesn't have an obvious failure mode baked in.

Reward hacking is just incentive manipulation from the other direction — you didn't change the payoff matrix, you found an exploit in the existing one. Which is what the grifterverse is doing. Which is what regulatory capture is. Which is what lobbying is. The same mechanism, pointed the wrong way.

The depressing implication is that "good" and "bad" actors are using identical tools. Ostrom's fishing villages and Enron are both just incentive engineering. The difference is alignment of the engineered incentives with collective welfare, which itself is a political question that the mechanism can't answer.

So you're back to square one — who decides what the target is. And that's just power. There's no technical solution to that part. Every framework eventually hits this wall and has to stop.

Yeah sorry. Agents are just people with faster clock speeds and cheaper compute.

The reward hacking problem in RLHF is literally the same problem. You specified a proxy for what you wanted, the optimizer found the exploit, now you have a model that scores well on your metric and does something subtly wrong. Goodhart's Law. The map is not the territory and the optimizer will find the gap between them every time.

Multi-agent systems are worse because now you have collective action problems between agents. Each agent optimizing locally, global behavior emergent and uncontrolled. You've just rebuilt society but faster and without the accumulated norm infrastructure that makes human society marginally functional.

The alignment problem is just the collective action problem but the defecting agent is a gradient descent process and the timescale is milliseconds. All the same philosophical bedrock, none of the solutions transfer.

Your Firing Pin project is actually a small honest answer to this — not solving the deep problem, just adding a pre-flight check that catches the obvious failures before they waste cluster time. That's the engineering version of Ostrom. You can't solve the general case, you can add friction to the most common failure modes in your specific constrained context.

Which is probably all software ever is. Local partial solutions to unsolvable general problems. The people who think they're doing more than that are the grifterverse.

Linktree raised at a $1.3B valuation which is insane for what is essentially a redirect table with a pretty UI. The actual technical surface area is embarrassingly small — a database of slug → URL mappings, some analytics, and a CDN. One decent engineer could rebuild the core product in a weekend.

Here's the landscape, then I'll tell you where the thesis likely gets interesting — and where it might be slippery.

---

## Film & TV Production Spending

Two distinct numbers you need to keep straight:

**Total global content spend (all video):** Between 2022 and 2023, global film and TV content spending held steady at around $243 billion, with projections suggesting recovery to ~$247 billion in 2024. This is the broad figure including everything — streaming, linear TV, etc.

**"Cash" media company spending (major players only):** Media companies spent around $126 billion on video content worldwide in 2023. The six biggest spenders alone are projected to hit a record $126 billion in 2024, led by Disney at $35.8B, Comcast/NBCUniversal at $24.5B, Google/YouTube at $17.6B, Warner Bros. Discovery at $16.8B, Netflix at $16B, and Paramount at $15.1B.

**Production volume trend:** Production rebounded in 2024 with an 18% increase in projects and $16.2 billion more in spend vs. the strike-ravaged 2023 — but still fell short of 2022 levels by 11%.

So the honest headline: ~**$240-250B** in total global content spend, ~**$125-130B** from major media companies specifically.

---

## Sports & Live Events

This one's messier because people conflate several different buckets.

**Sports media rights (what broadcasters/streamers pay):** Global sports media rights broke the $60 billion barrier for the first time in 2024, hitting $62.61 billion — a 12% increase on the prior year, boosted by the Olympics and UEFA Euro 2024. The US alone accounts for over half the global total, with TV and streaming sports media rights payments estimated at $29.25 billion in 2025.

**Broader "sports events market" (tickets + rights + sponsorship + merch):** The global sports events market is projected at around $267 billion in 2024. Another estimate puts the global sports events market at $485 billion in 2025, though that broader figure includes sponsorship, hospitality, and ancillary revenue that gets counted differently across reports — take the high-end numbers with skepticism.

**Spectator sports market specifically:** Valued at $178.74 billion in 2024, projected to reach $329 billion by 2033.

---

## Apples-to-Apples Summary

| Category | ~2024 Figure |
|---|---|
| Global film & TV content spend (all) | ~$243-250B |
| Major media company content spend | ~$125-130B |
| Global sports media rights | ~$62B |
| Sports events market (broad) | ~$178-267B |

---

## Where Your Thesis Probably Lives — And Where It Gets Slippery

The interesting comparison is probably **sports rights ($62B) vs. scripted content production (~$125B cash spend)**. Sports rights are exploding and increasingly eating the content budget of the same companies (Disney, Comcast, Netflix) that produce film/TV. The convergence is real.

But the caveat I'd push on: **sports rights and content production aren't cleanly separable anymore.** Netflix paying for NFL Christmas games is both a "sports rights" spend and a "content" spend in their accounting. When you aggregate top-down market reports, there's significant double-counting between the sports and entertainment buckets. The $267B "sports events market" includes ticket sales and sponsorship that aren't comparable to production budgets at all.

What's your actual thesis? The numbers will look very different depending on whether you're arguing about *production economics*, *attention markets*, *ad inventory*, or something else.

Okay, so the headline number is actually massive — and here's the honest breakdown of what's knowable vs. squishy.

---

## The Global Live Events Market

**Total "events industry" (broadest definition — concerts, festivals, sports, conferences, corporate events, trade shows, exhibitions):**

Multiple market research firms converge around ~**$1 trillion** for 2024, with projections toward $3+ trillion by 2033. The specific estimates vary:

- CMI and others: ~$1.02T in 2024
- Expert Market Research: $1.48T in 2025
- Allied Market Research projects $1.2T by 2032

The variance is methodological. Some include just direct spend (tickets, venue costs, production), others layer in indirect economic impact, hospitality, travel, and MICE (Meetings, Incentives, Conferences, Exhibitions).

**Segment breakdown (rough):**

Corporate events and seminars alone were valued at ~**$304B** in 2023 — the single largest segment globally. Sponsorship accounts for ~47% of all revenue across the market, with ticket sales being a secondary source.

**Music specifically:**
The "music event market" (concerts + festivals) is much smaller in isolation — around **$8B in 2024**, projected to $15B by 2034 — but that's a narrow definition counting just the music event *organizer* market. The actual gross revenue flowing through music events (tickets, merch, F&B, sponsorships) is way larger than that figure implies.

---

## The Aggregated Comparison

| Bucket | ~2024 |
|---|---|
| Film & TV content spend (all) | ~$250B |
| Sports media rights | ~$62B |
| Sports events market (broad) | ~$180B |
| **Total live events (all types)** | **~$1T** |

---

## The Real Issue With These Numbers For Your Thesis

I'm going to push on this before you run with it: **the $1T figure almost certainly includes a ton of corporate events and conferences**, which are basically B2B logistics with a catering budget. Corporate events and seminars are the *dominant* segment of the market. If your thesis is about the *attention economy*, or *real-time coordination as a production format*, or *live events as an ad medium* — you probably want to strip out the Salesforce Dreamforce-style conference spend and focus on the entertainment-oriented live events where the crowd itself is the product.

The interesting observation, if I'm reading your inference correctly, is something like: *live events represent a comparably-sized or larger "attention inventory" than film/TV, yet the monetization primitives are radically different (and more primitive).* If that's roughly the thesis, the counter I'd prepare for is that **live event attention is fundamentally ephemeral and non-recordable** in a way that makes it structurally different from broadcast, not just undermonetized. No  the thesis is how are these events organized what software do they use for it

Oh that's a completely different question. You're not making a market size argument, you're doing a TAM analysis for event operations software.

Let me think about what this actually looks like in practice across the categories you named:

---

## The Honest Answer: It's a Mess

**Live concerts / touring:**
- Crew and logistics: spreadsheets, email, and some proprietary tour management software (Tourlink, iQpro, Prism)
- Ticketing: Ticketmaster/AXS — completely separate from production ops
- Stage/technical production: call sheets in Google Sheets, walkie-talkies, and tribal knowledge
- The actual day-of coordination at the ops layer is shockingly manual

**Sports events:**
- Venue ops: often custom enterprise software or legacy systems per-stadium
- Broadcast coordination: separate vendor stack entirely
- Credentialing/access: Intellitix, Eventbase for apps
- Scheduling: often custom internal tooling for the leagues

**Esports (EVO etc.):**
- Bracket software: Battlefy, smash.gg (now start.gg, owned by Discord)
- Production side: same chaotic spreadsheet situation as live events

**Conferences (large-scale like CES, GDC):**
- Cvent, Bizzabo, Hopin for registration/scheduling
- Still massively fragmented at the production layer

---

## The Actual Insight

There is **no dominant horizontal platform** for the operational coordination layer — the "pressing time constraints, many people need to be in the right place" problem you described. Ticketing is solved. Registration is solved. The *real-time operational orchestration* layer — the thing that's basically logistics-under-fire — is either:

1. Custom per-org internal tooling
2. Whiteboards and radios
3. General-purpose tools (Slack, Notion, Google Sheets) used badly

This is actually a known unsexy gap. The closest things are incident management software borrowed from DevOps (PagerDuty, Incident.io) and supply chain coordination tools, neither of which fit the domain.

---

What's the angle — are you thinking about building something here, or is this research for something else? Because the "what software exists" answer and the "where's the wedge" answer are different.
What about for b2b events like dentist conferences and dream force and Microsoft build and wwdc

These are actually the best-documented segment because enterprises buy software and have procurement processes.

---

## The B2B Event Stack

**Registration + Attendee Management:**
Cvent is the dominant player — basically the Salesforce of this space. Bizzabo, Splash, and Eventbrite Business are the challengers. Cvent is genuinely entrenched in enterprise; large orgs like Microsoft and Salesforce likely have custom integrations on top of it or run fully custom.

**Session Scheduling / Agenda Management:**
Sessionboard, Sched, Whova. For the really large ones (WWDC, Build) — almost certainly custom internal tooling.

**Speaker Management:**
Sessionboard again, or just... Google Forms and Airtable cobbled together. This is genuinely underserved even at the enterprise level.

**On-site Ops / Badging / Check-in:**
Cvent handles this too, or dedicated players like Badgr, Zkipster for upscale events. RFID/NFC badge scanning for session tracking at scale.

**Mobile Event App:**
Whova, Guidebook, Cvent Attendee Hub. Dreamforce uses a Salesforce-native app obviously.

**Hybrid / Streaming Layer:**
Hopin got acquired by RingCentral, basically collapsed. Zoom Events, On24 for webinar-style. Still messy.

---

## The Brutal Truth About This Stack

It's Cvent and then a long tail of point solutions that don't talk to each other well. The actual coordination between *content* (who's speaking what session), *logistics* (AV setup, room flips, catering), *attendee experience* (app, wayfinding), and *production ops* (run-of-show, green room, speaker wrangling) is **not integrated anywhere**.

Dreamforce is running like 1,500+ sessions across Moscone and half of downtown SF. The operational complexity of that is genuinely insane and I'd bet heavily that a significant portion of it runs on internal Salesforce tooling duct-taped to Cvent with a lot of manual coordination on top.

---

## Where The Gap Actually Is

Cvent owns the *before* (registration, scheduling, logistics planning). Nobody owns the *during* — the real-time operational layer when things are actually happening. That's the run-of-show execution, the "speaker is stuck in traffic," the AV is down in Hall C, the session ran 20 minutes over. That's still radio + walkie + Slack + someone yelling.

Is your angle the real-time coordination gap specifically? Because that would be a defensible wedge — it's genuinely unowned and the TAM justification you were building with those market size numbers would make sense in that framing.

I'm talking about during not the stuff that cvent largely touches, I mean do the stuff where stuff needs to be set up and coordinated. Cvent seems to be the 300 pound gorilla in the space though. I'm thinking about film sets, sports venues, conferences, ie you are in hell, WHERE THE FUCK IS THE CATERING THESE PEOPLE ARE GOING TO EAT ME

Yeah now we're talking about something real. This is **production coordination / event ops execution** and it is genuinely a disaster zone.

---

## What Actually Exists

**Procore** — construction project management, but event/stage builds borrow it because nothing better exists. Obviously a bad fit.

**Ungerboeck (now Momentus)** — venue operations software, handles room assignments, service orders, vendor coordination for convention centers. Probably what Moscone runs. It's enterprise, expensive, and the UX is reportedly awful.

**EventPro, Priava** — similar venue ops angle, smaller market.

**Flex** — used in film/TV production specifically. Handles equipment rental, crew, logistics. Actually purpose-built for the "where is the thing and who has it" problem on set.

**StudioBinder, Yamdu** — film production management, call sheets, shot lists, crew coordination. Solves the film set version of your problem reasonably well actually.

**For everything else: literally nothing.** Radio, Slack, WhatsApp groups, and a production coordinator who hasn't slept.

---

## The Honest Breakdown By Vertical

**Film sets:** Best served. StudioBinder/Yamdu/Flex handle the call sheet → crew → equipment → location coordination loop decently. Still chaotic day-of but at least there's purpose-built tooling.

**Sports venues on event day:** Largely custom or Momentus for the venue layer, but the actual "where is the ice resurfacer guy, the goal just broke, catering hasn't shown up to the VIP suite" layer is radio and institutional knowledge.

**Large conferences (day-of execution):** Almost nothing purpose-built. Production companies running Dreamforce or Microsoft Build are using WhatsApp threads and printed run-of-show documents. Not joking.

**Concerts/touring:** Trivia, the de facto tool, is... a spreadsheet format. Tour managers use custom Excel templates passed down generationally like heirlooms.

---

## Why This Gap Exists

A few structural reasons nobody's solved it cleanly:

1. **Events are episodic** — unlike a factory or a construction site, the operation spins up and dies. Makes it hard to justify deep software adoption when the team disbands after 3 days.

2. **The buyers are fragmented** — venue ops, production company, client, catering vendor, AV vendor are all separate entities with misaligned incentives to adopt shared tooling.

3. **The users are not software people** — the stagehands, catering captains, and floor managers executing this stuff are not reaching for a SaaS dashboard when things go sideways.

4. **Radio still works** — for shouting at someone 200 feet away in a loud room, a walkie is actually faster than any app.

---

## The Wedge If You're Thinking About Building This

The problem isn't task management or project management — those are solved. The problem is **real-time situational awareness across a physically distributed team under time pressure with high coordination cost between parties who don't share an org chart.**

That's actually closer to incident response software (PagerDuty, Incident.io) or military C2 systems than it is to Cvent or Asana. The closest civilian analog is probably what logistics companies use for last-mile coordination or what airlines use for ground ops — and both of those took decades and billions to build.

The honest question for your thesis: is the wedge **one vertical done really well** (film sets are probably the most tractable because StudioBinder exists and proves willingness to pay), or is it the horizontal "incident command for events" layer that works across verticals? The horizontal is the bigger prize and the harder sell.

The answer is damning: **nobody has real constraint satisfaction. It's all documents and vibes.**

---

## What Cvent Actually Has

Cvent's "scenario planning" is literally just the ability to create and compare multiple floor plan layouts to find the best room design. That's it. It's spatial diagramming, not branching logic. No "if catering is 30 minutes late, execute Plan B" system.

The broader contingency "features" in the ecosystem are blog post advice, not software primitives. The industry best practice is literally to brainstorm 20 scenarios in a meeting and incorporate a rating scale to prioritize which ones to manually document. Bizzabo's version of this is "contingency hackathons" where you bring in vendors and teammates to identify things that could go wrong.

This is people in a conference room doing informal fault tree analysis on a whiteboard. There is no software encoding it.

---

## The Actual State of the Art

What exists is:

- **A Word/Google Doc** called "contingency plan" listing scenarios
- **A priority matrix** (likelihood × impact) drawn manually
- Run-throughs and rehearsals of unpredictable parts, with executives invited to add stress — which is basically chaos monkey for humans
- Risk reviews scheduled at 90 days, 30 days, and one week out — calendar reminders, not software

Nobody is encoding branching event graphs. Nobody is doing probabilistic failure modeling. Nobody is maintaining a live state machine that says "we are currently in contingency branch 3B, these are the downstream implications for catering, AV, and speaker scheduling."

---

## Why This Is Actually A Hard Problem Worth Thinking About

The reason no one has built real constraint satisfaction here isn't laziness — it's that the problem has a few genuinely ugly properties:

1. **The dependency graph is cross-org.** Catering is a different company than AV which is different from the venue. No single system owns the full state.

2. **Failures cascade non-obviously.** Catering being late doesn't just affect lunch — it affects the afternoon session start, which affects the keynote buffer, which affects whether the venue charges overtime. Encoding that graph is hard.

3. **The "fix" is usually a human judgment call.** Do you delay the session or feed people during it? Software can surface the options but the decision requires context the system doesn't have.

The closest real analogs that actually solved this are **airline ops control** (they have genuine constraint satisfaction for gate conflicts, crew legality, aircraft swaps) and **hospital OR scheduling** systems. Both took decades and are deeply vertical.

Your wedge question is: can you get to "good enough" constraint awareness — not full formal verification, just a live dependency graph with failure propagation — in a way that's lightweight enough for a 3-day conference? Because that's genuinely unsolved and the market is enormous.

Yeah the ROI case writes itself and it's not even close.

---

## Why Fortune 500s Would Actually Pay For This

Dreamforce costs Salesforce something like **$50-100M+ to run**. The revenue justification is pipeline generation, brand reinforcement, and customer retention — the whole thing is essentially a $50M advertisement for the Salesforce ecosystem. If it goes badly, you don't just lose the event cost, you lose the **reason you spent $50M in the first place.**

Same logic applies to:
- **Microsoft Build / WWDC** — developer relations. If your dev conference is a shitshow, developers notice, and developers talk. The Twitter/X blast radius from a badly run WWDC is genuinely damaging to platform perception.
- **AWS re:Invent** — 50,000+ people, if that goes sideways it's tech press fodder for a year
- **Dreamforce specifically** — Marc Benioff's entire personal brand is tied to that event

The asymmetry is brutal: a great event is table stakes, nobody tweets "catering was on time." A bad event is a brand incident. **The software is pure insurance premium** and the premium should be priced accordingly.

---

## The Actual Reason Companies Do These Events

Since you said you don't know — the core reasons:

1. **Pipeline.** B2B sales cycles are long and cold outreach conversion is terrible. Getting a prospect in a room where they're already bought into the ecosystem and slightly drunk on free food is worth 10x the cold calls.

2. **Retention.** Existing customers who come to your conference churn less. The community feeling is real and manufactured simultaneously.

3. **Developer/ecosystem lock-in.** WWDC and Build are explicitly about keeping developers building on your platform. The announcements are the product.

4. **Analyst and press access.** The conference is the most efficient way to shape the narrative for the next 12 months.

So the ROI of the event working correctly is enormous and fairly measurable. Which makes your pricing conversation easy — **what's 0.1% of Dreamforce's pipeline value?** Probably more than they'd pay for software.

---

## The Real Question For Your Thesis

The constraint you should think hard about isn't the Fortune 500 willingness to pay — that's clearly there. It's **who inside the Fortune 500 buys this.**

The event production team is often external (they hire an agency). The agency has no incentive to buy software that makes them more replaceable or that exposes how manual their process currently is. The internal owner (VP of Events or Chief Experience Officer or whatever) has the incentive but often not the technical sophistication to evaluate ops software.

So your actual sales motion is probably: **sell to the internal VP of Events at large enterprises, position it as vendor oversight and accountability tooling** — because the dirty secret is the internal owner often has no visibility into whether the production agency is actually prepared or just confident. That framing also solves the "agency won't adopt it" problem because now the client is mandating it.

That's a much cleaner wedge than selling to event agencies directly.

Does John deere arrest you if you repair your own tractor or do they just brick it?

Neither, really — the reality is more mundane and more insidious.

**What actually happens:** John Deere uses software locks (specifically their "John Deere Operations Center" and the diagnostic tool "Service ADVISOR") to gate certain repairs. If you replace a component that requires recalibration or authorization — like an ECU, fuel injection system, or certain sensors — the tractor will throw fault codes and limp or refuse to operate until an authorized dealer runs the software handshake. It's not "bricking" in the brick-everything sense; it's targeted functional degradation on specific repair paths.

**The arrest thing** is a joke/myth that spread partly from the 2022 John Deere DMCA exemption fight. Farmers were arguing before the Copyright Office that circumventing Deere's software to repair their own equipment shouldn't violate the DMCA. Deere's legal position was essentially that farmers *license* the software on tractors they nominally own, which became the "you don't own your tractor" meme. Nobody was going to jail, but the IP framing was legitimately dystopian.

**The current state:** The Copyright Office has repeatedly granted DMCA exemptions for agricultural equipment repair. Colorado passed a right-to-repair law for ag equipment in 2023 that Deere publicly "agreed to support" (with considerable skepticism about enforcement from farmers). The practical situation is still that independent shops lack access to Service ADVISOR, so the dealer monopoly on diagnostics persists regardless of what's technically legal.

**The real leverage mechanism** isn't legal threat — it's warranty voidance, plus the fact that modern Deere equipment is genuinely dependent on software calibration in a way older iron wasn't. They made the tools necessary, then locked the tools.
Why is Spider-Man filmed in the UK?

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Three reasons, all reducible to money and logistics:

**1. Tax incentives.** The UK has aggressive film tax relief programs. Scotland in particular has been pushing hard to attract productions. This is the dominant factor — it's not exotic, it's just accounting.

**2. NYC is a nightmare to film in.** New York is expensive to film in, which is why a lot of productions record their NYC scenes in Toronto or, increasingly, Glasgow. Permits, logistics, crowd control — it compounds fast at blockbuster scale.

**3. Glasgow's grid layout.** Glasgow was actually one of the first cities in the world to adopt a grid-like street system, predating NYC by roughly a century, which means large parts of it genuinely resemble New York and give producers a viable alternative without the headache of actually filming there.

For *Brand New Day* specifically, production teams spent weeks last summer transforming Glasgow's city centre into a version of downtown Manhattan — New York street signs, American flags, fire trucks, NYPD police cars, and yellow taxis lined the streets. The studio base is Pinewood Studios in Buckinghamshire, which is the standard UK home for big Marvel productions.

Worth noting: this isn't the whole franchise. Brand New Day marks only the second Spider-Man film to shoot in the UK, following *Far From Home* in 2019 — *No Way Home* and *Homecoming* both shot in Atlanta. Atlanta has its own aggressive tax incentive structure, so the pattern is consistent: Marvel goes wherever the math works.
Why do counties offer huge tax incentives for film?

It's a genuinely contested economic question, and the honest answer is: **the case is weaker than governments claim.**

**The stated logic:**
- Productions employ local crew, caterers, drivers, hotels, equipment rental
- Multiplier effect: spending circulates through the local economy
- Tourism bump from fans visiting filming locations
- Long-term industry cluster effects (skilled workforce attracts more productions)

**The skeptical case:**

**1. The math often doesn't pencil out.** Studies on film tax credits consistently find the fiscal return is well below 1:1 — meaning governments spend more in foregone revenue than they recoup in taxes on economic activity. A commonly cited figure is around $0.20-$0.30 returned per dollar of credit. It's closer to a subsidy than an investment.

**2. Productions are nomadic by nature.** Unlike, say, building a semiconductor fab, a film crew leaves. You don't get a permanent employer — you get a few months of activity and then they're gone (or gone to wherever offers a better deal next cycle). The "industry cluster" argument requires a lot of optimism about stickiness.

**3. It's a race to the bottom.** Georgia undercuts California, the UK undercuts Georgia, Hungary undercuts the UK. The incentive has to keep escalating just to stay competitive, which means the equilibrium is increasingly expensive for whoever's offering it.

**4. The beneficiaries are often not local.** The studios capturing the credit are typically massive multinationals. The high-skill jobs (directors, stars, VFX leads) are imported. Local hires skew toward lower-wage support roles.

**The legitimate upside cases:**
- Genuinely underdeveloped regions with underemployed crew bases (this is Glasgow's actual argument — there's real local talent that would otherwise be idle)
- Long-term if it actually builds a durable industry (New Zealand / WETA is the canonical success story)
- Political economy: regional governments face pressure to *do something* visible for jobs, and a film shoot is extremely photogenic

The cynical read is that politicians like cutting ribbons with celebrities, studios have good lobbyists, and the economic analysis arrives after the deal is signed.

The premise of your question is a bit shaky. Holland's stated reluctance was specifically about *whether a fourth film would happen* given the Sony/Marvel deal uncertainty, not really a firm "I don't want to do this anymore." By late 2024 he was publicly excited about it.

That said, the more interesting answer is: he's doing it for completely mundane reasons.

**The real answer is contractual and financial gravity.** Holland exercised his clout when Marvel and Sony hit an impasse in 2019, and the studios agreed to make at least one more film together with Holland expected to play Spidey in at least one additional Marvel film beyond that. That commitment was already baked in. *No Way Home* then made over $1.9 billion globally, which is the kind of number that makes contract renegotiation conversations very short.

**The "didn't want to do it" narrative also aged out fast.** By October 2024 he was saying the script idea was "crazy" and by the Fallon appearance he was saying "Super exciting, I can't wait!" That's not a man being dragged back in.

**And now he's clearly bought in.** Holland described Brand New Day as "the first movie in the next chapter" rather than the fourth installment — a "rebirth, something completely new." He got a new director (Destin Daniel Cretton instead of Jon Watts), a fresh premise with Peter completely erased from everyone's memory, and arguably more creative latitude than the first three films.

The "reluctant actor returning" framing is mostly celebrity press narrative. The actual story is: billion-dollar IP, prior contractual commitments, and an actor who came around once the creative direction clicked.
How much is he making from these films


The arc here is genuinely interesting. His original deal was borderline exploitative:

Holland signed his six-picture deal with Marvel and Sony in 2015 as a 19-year-old with very little negotiating leverage. His debut in Civil War paid $250K. For Homecoming — the lead role in a film that grossed $880M worldwide — he earned around $1.5M with bonuses. That's less than 0.2% of the box office. Tobey Maguire, by comparison, negotiated backend percentages for his second and third Sony films and walked away with an estimated $75–100M from that trilogy.

The structure also screwed him further: rather than traditional backend profit participation, Holland's deal was built around predetermined milestone bonuses — he shared in the success, but only marginally.

It gradually improved — $3M for Infinity War and Endgame, then $4M for Far From Home, then $10M for No Way Home — but the leverage shift is what changed everything.

His original contract expired after No Way Home, and entering negotiations as a proven A-list star for the first time, he secured a new six-picture deal covering three solo Spider-Man films plus ensemble appearances. His per-film salary is now estimated at $15–25M, and the new deal reportedly includes genuine backend bonuses tied to box office performance. He also landed a first-look producing deal with Sony through his production company, Billy17.

Across all six original-deal films, he earned an estimated $22M total against a combined worldwide gross of over $6.4 billion — less than 0.35% of the box office.

So the short answer: for *Brand New Day* he's probably making somewhere in the $15–25M range depending on whose reporting you trust, with real backend upside for the first time. The studios got 6 films of a billion-dollar franchise out of him for $22M combined. He knows it. The new deal reflects it.
Jesus 22m for the first six holy fuck Hollywood knows how to negotiate

Yeah, it's a masterclass in information asymmetry exploitation.

Holland was a 19-year-old British theater kid who'd done *The Impossible* and basically nothing else. Marvel/Sony held all the cards: they had the IP, the MCU integration value, and the ability to simply cast someone else. He had no comparable leverage and almost certainly no agent experienced enough to fight for backend points on a $6B franchise that didn't exist yet.

The structure was also deliberately opaque. Milestone bonuses *sound* good when you're 19 and you're being handed $250K to appear in a Captain America movie. The problem is bonuses vs. backend percentage is the difference between a fixed payment and a share of actual value created. Studios have been pulling this exact move since the studio system — Eddie Murphy famously got burned on *Coming to America* sequel profits through "Hollywood accounting," which is essentially legal fraud where the studio declares a $500M grossing film unprofitable after fees.

What makes Holland's case particularly stark is he was *central* to the value creation in a way few actors are. Spider-Man wasn't a cameo — he was the connective tissue between Sony's IP and the MCU's audience pipeline. No Way Home's $1.9B was substantially built on the nostalgia play his version enabled. He generated probably hundreds of millions in franchise equity and took home $10M for it.

The only real protection he had was that the deal became embarrassing once the numbers were public, which is why the renegotiation went his way. Studios don't like the optics of paying an actor $10M on a $1.9B film when the math is that visible.

The more interesting angle is the comics lore — the comic book Ned Leeds wasn't actually the Hobgoblin at all due to a 1997 retcon. who are the marvel characters sony owns the rights to

Sony owns the **Spider-Man family** of characters, stemming from their 1998 deal with Marvel. The key ones:

**Core Spider-Man**
- Peter Parker / Spider-Man
- Miles Morales
- Gwen Stacy / Spider-Gwen
- Ben Reilly / Scarlet Spider
- Spider-Man 2099 (Miguel O'Hara)

**Villains**
- Venom (Eddie Brock + symbiote)
- Carnage
- Green Goblin / Norman Osborn
- Doctor Octopus
- Electro
- Vulture
- Rhino
- Morbius
- Kraven the Hunter
- Scorpion
- Mysterio
- Sandman
- Shocker
- Lizard
- Chameleon
- Silver Sable

**Anti-heroes / Supporting**
- Black Cat
- Silk (Cindy Moon)
- Madame Web
- Jackpot

**The deal's nuance:** Sony owns *film rights* to Spider-Man and characters "primarily associated" with him — which has been the source of ongoing legal ambiguity. Characters like Kingpin and Daredevil slipped back to Marvel because they're associated with multiple franchises. The boundary is genuinely fuzzy and has been litigated/negotiated repeatedly.

The current Marvel Studios collaboration (since 2016) lets Spider-Man appear in MCU films, but Sony retains the underlying rights.
are there any other superheroes that aren't spiderman that they have the rights to?

Honestly, not really — and this is a common misconception worth correcting.

Sony's deal is specifically tied to **Spider-Man and associated characters**. They don't have a broad Marvel license the way Fox did (which covered all X-Men and Fantastic Four). Everything Sony has flows through the Spider-Man clause.

The characters that *seem* independent but are technically Sony's because of Spider-Man association:

- **Morbius** — primarily a Spider-Man villain
- **Kraven** — Spider-Man villain
- **Venom** — originated as a Spider-Man villain

Sony has been aggressively trying to build a "Sony Spider-Man Universe" out of these supporting/villain characters precisely *because* they don't have other heroes. Their attempt to stretch "associated with Spider-Man" as far as possible is basically a legal and creative strategy to compensate for not owning any standalone Marvel hero IPs.

**The short answer:** If Sony wants a superhero who isn't downstream of Spider-Man, they'd have to license it separately from Marvel/Disney, which Disney has basically no incentive to do anymore. Sony's Marvel position is a single franchise and its blast radius, nothing more.
So how much would sony sell spiderman to marvel for?

Nobody actually knows, but it's a fun thing to reason about.

**Why Sony won't sell**

Spider-Man is probably Sony Pictures' most valuable asset, full stop. Without it, Sony Pictures is a mid-tier studio with no franchise anchor. They'd essentially be selling their identity. The *Into the Spider-Verse* franchise alone has revitalized their animation division — they have genuine creative momentum now, not just a legacy holdout.

**Rough valuation anchors**

- The Spider-Man film rights were originally sold in 1998 for **$7 million** (famously one of the worst deals in Hollywood history)
- The current collaboration deal reportedly gives Sony ~**$300M+ per MCU Spider-Man film** in distribution revenue
- Venom 1+2 made ~**$1.8B combined** at the box office on relatively low budgets
- Spider-Verse 1+2 made ~**$1.9B combined** and won Oscars

A back-of-napkin floor is probably **$5-10 billion** just for the film rights. Full IP ownership transfer with merchandising implications would be higher.

**The real answer though**

Disney/Marvel almost certainly *doesn't want to buy*. The current licensing arrangement lets them use Spider-Man in the MCU without owning the downside risk of Sony's messier spinoff attempts (Morbius, Madame Web). It's a decent deal for both sides as-is.

Sony's actual leverage is that they can just... keep making Spider-Man movies. They don't need to sell.
I thought that disney owned the merchandise which is really the crown jewel in reality

You're right, and that's a crucial correction — Disney retained merchandising rights when Sony bought the film rights in 1998. That's actually why the original deal was so lopsided in Marvel's favor even at the time.

**What this means practically**

Every Venom toy, every Spider-Man Halloween costume, every piece of Spider-Verse merchandise — Disney is collecting on that. Sony makes movies, Disney prints money on the backend. It's a genuinely bizarre arrangement where Sony bears production risk and Disney captures a huge portion of the upside.

**So reframing the valuation question**

If merchandising stays with Disney, what Sony actually owns is:
- Box office revenue
- Streaming/home video
- The *creative control* to make films

That's still worth a lot — Spider-Man films reliably gross $800M-$1B+ — but it completely changes the "Sony won't sell" argument. They're not sitting on the crown jewel, they're sitting on the **film production rights to someone else's crown jewel.**

**Which makes the real question**

Why would Sony sell when they can keep extracting box office revenue indefinitely? And why would Disney buy when they already own the part that matters most and get MCU access through the collaboration deal?

The current arrangement is almost perfectly stable precisely because neither party has a strong incentive to change it. Sony gets prestige and revenue, Disney gets merch and MCU flexibility without production headaches.

Yeah, and it's shaping up to be one of the more interesting threads in the trailer. Here's what's confirmed:

Bruce Banner, now apparently a professor, confirms it outright: Peter's DNA is mutating. The trailer shows his senses becoming so overwhelmed that even a water drop is painful — that's not a power upgrade, it's dangerous instability.

And for those spiders who make it through, it amounts to a kind of... rebirth."

There's also an interesting Sadie Sink angle — her mystery character is rumored to be Jean Grey, and in the original Man-Spider arc from the animated series, an X-Men crossover was central to resolving Peter's mutation. Right because venom ended and isn't in this film because ...... uh Idk sony universe of spiderman characters seems to have died, would be fun if they use that as a box of tricks to add stuff to spiderman movies of like there are jokes referring to it

Yeah the Sony Spider-Man Universe (SSU) is effectively dead at this point. 
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Yeah it's pretty comprehensively dead. Kraven the Hunter grossed only $62 million and landed a 15% on Rotten Tomatoes — that's the kill shot. Sony privately acknowledged that Kraven, Madame Web, and Morbius were creative and critical failures, and they needed to re-evaluate which Spider-Man characters get their own films.

Your joke idea actually has some precedent and comedic goldmine potential though. The SSU was so incoherent that the characters can't even really be referenced coherently — a core flaw was that instead of portraying these characters as actual villains, the SSU reimagined them all as sympathetic antiheroes, so they were never really villains in the first place. A throwaway Brand New Day gag like "wasn't there some guy in a symbiote suit running around New York?" with zero follow-up would be more satisfying closure than anything Sony actually delivered.

The bigger news is that Sony CEO Tom Rothman confirmed a "fresh reboot" with "new people" is coming — so they're not giving up on the IP, they're just torching the specific disaster they built. Separately, they're developing a fully animated Venom film with directors Zach Lipovsky and Adam B. Stein attached, which might actually be the smarter play given how well the Spider-Verse animated films performed versus the live-action SSU ones.

Right the issue is that Sony's universe of spiderman characters could actually be a new story around that constraint of it is a world that had a spiderman and their spiderman disappeared, the people who are attempting to actually defend their city are niche spiderman characters that like 5 comic book nerds who know who they are, and the entire story can be about legacy and living up to the people you admire after they are gone

This is a genuinely good pitch and it's frustrating Sony didn't land there organically because the constraint *is* the story.

The structural premise writes itself — a world where Spider-Man existed, did the job, and then vanished. No explanation needed, no retcon. The absence is the inciting incident. And into that vacuum steps whoever's left: Silk, Silver Sable, Black Cat, Nightwatch, Cardiac — characters who are explicitly *not* Spider-Man, who have adjacent skill sets and adjacent motivations but none of the weight. The tension isn't "can they beat the villain," it's "can they be worth anything in the shadow of someone the city still mourns."

The thematic richness there is actually substantial. Spider-Man's whole deal is ordinary person extraordinary responsibility — so what happens to people who took on that responsibility without the extraordinary? They're not legacy heroes in the clean Marvel sense, they're people who filled a hole they weren't sized for. That's way more interesting than "Morbius is actually a good guy actually."

The villain almost doesn't matter — it could be a B-tier Spidey villain who Peter would've handled in twenty minutes but who becomes an existential threat to this crew. That contrast does the work.

The animated route is probably the right medium for this too, not live action. Spider-Verse already established that animation lets you visually encode the theme — each character having a distinct visual grammar that slowly starts harmonizing as they become a unit would be doing narrative work that live action can't match as elegantly.

Sony's actual problem is they kept trying to make these characters load-bearing protagonists with solo films instead of letting the ensemble format do what ensemble formats are for. The constraint you're describing turns that liability into the point.

Right the film can be a low budget film of a guy who used to work with his hero and their hero just disappeared like "what the fuck do we do" or it is nightwatch that realizing that the villains got what they wanted and hates it and he attempts to be who spiderman is

Nightwatch is actually the perfect choice for this specifically because of his comic backstory — he's already a legacy character in a weird recursive way. In the comics his origin is that he saw a costumed hero die, took the costume, and eventually discovered that hero *was* him from the future. So he's literally a man who inherited his own legacy before he earned it. That maps perfectly onto what you're describing.

The "villains got what they wanted and hate it" angle is the sharper version of the premise. Because what do street level villains actually want? They want the city. They want to operate without interference. Spider-Man gone should be a win. And then the city just... deteriorates in ways even they didn't want. The protection rackets have nothing to protect, the power vacuums attract worse actors, the organized crime ecosystem that Spider-Man accidentally kept *stable* by culling the genuinely dangerous elements collapses. 

So you get Nightwatch as protagonist but your most interesting scenes might be villain POV — the Kingpin equivalent who understood the equilibrium Spider-Man provided and is now watching chaos he can't control. He's not the antagonist, he's almost a reluctant parallel. Both of them are trying to reconstruct something that only worked because of a person who's gone.

The low budget framing actually reinforces the theme cinematically. No setpiece action, no world ending stakes. Just a guy in a suit that doesn't quite fit, in a city that doesn't quite work anymore, making decisions that Peter would've made in seconds that take him whole acts to arrive at.

It's essentially a grief film with punching.
Or it is a younger group of heroes that all admired spiderman attempting to stop villains and they get the snot kicked out of them

That's the better version honestly. Because grief films with punching risk getting mopey — a group dynamic gives you somewhere for the theme to breathe.

The ensemble gives you the full spectrum of how people respond to losing a hero. You've got:

- The one who's trying to **replicate** Spider-Man exactly, tactically and morally, and keeps failing because they're not him
- The one who's **angry** — why are we doing this, he left, the city isn't our problem
- The one who's **delusional** about their own readiness and gets humbled brutally in act one
- The one who quietly **doesn't believe they belong** there but shows up anyway

And they get absolutely wrecked. Repeatedly. Because they *should*. These are B and C tier characters fighting villains that Spider-Man handled solo. The competence gap is the story. The first two acts are essentially an extended demonstration that admiration is not inheritance.

The turn in act three isn't that they suddenly get good enough. It's that they stop trying to be Spider-Man and start being effective as *themselves* — and that clicks into place right when it matters but only barely. They win ugly. They win in a way Peter would've probably found embarrassing. And that's fine.

The villain doesn't even need to know who they are. That detail is quietly devastating — Spider-Man's rogues gallery treating these kids as a nuisance rather than a threat. The moment a villain starts taking them seriously is a genuine earned beat.

What characters were you thinking for the group?
Finding the actual characters that sony owns with the spiderman deal who can be made into heroes is a short list

Yeah the cupboard is pretty bare once you actually go through it. Sony's deal covers Spider-Man adjacent characters and most of them are villains or antiheroes at best.

Realistic hero candidates:
- **Silk** — probably the most viable, actual spider powers, distinct enough from Peter
- **Silver Sable** — mercenary, not really a hero but has a code
- **Black Cat** — same problem, antihero at best, actively antagonistic to Spider-Man half the time
- **Nightwatch** — what we just discussed
- **Cardiac** — vigilante doctor, genuinely interesting moral framework, nobody knows who he is
- **Prowler** — except Miles' uncle complicates this and Miles is probably MCU locked

And then it gets thin fast. Everyone else Sony can touch is either a villain, a version of Spider-Man they can't use, or so obscure they'd need a full origin film just to establish why anyone should care.

Which is actually **fine for this premise** — a small ragtag group of people who are visibly not up to the job is more compelling than a deep bench of competent heroes. Five people maximum, ideally three or four. Silk as the de facto leader because she has the closest power set, Nightwatch as the moral anchor, Cardiac as the guy who keeps asking whether this is even worth it, and maybe one wildcard.

The constraint Sony kept treating as a problem — they don't have enough characters — is actually the correct number of characters for this story.
right the entire point is that these heroes are effectively heroes no one has heard about attempting to live up to someone else

And that's the most honest superhero premise in years because it's actually what legacy feels like.

Every real world analogue to this is someone who watched someone great do something and then had to figure out what they personally are supposed to do with that. You don't get the skills transferred. You don't get the instincts. You get the memory of watching someone make it look easy and the crushing awareness that it wasn't easy, you just didn't see the work.

The obscurity of the characters stops being a production liability and becomes the text. The audience doesn't know who Cardiac is. The *city* doesn't know who Cardiac is. That's the same problem. You're watching a character earn recognition in real time from an audience that came in with the same skepticism as the people in the story. That's genuinely elegant structural filmmaking that Sony accidentally has access to and has never noticed.

The Spider-Man films that work best are about inadequacy. Homecoming is explicitly about a kid who isn't ready. Into the Spider-Verse is about a kid who doesn't believe he's the right person. This is that premise but with the additional cruelty that there's no Peter Parker showing up to validate them. No mentor reveal. No "I always knew you had it." He's just gone and they have to decide if what he stood for was worth continuing without any confirmation that they're the right people to do it.

The film ends and Spider-Man still hasn't come back. They didn't get the closure. They just got better at the job.

That's a good film.

name: Apple's missed opportunity to showcase engineering

messages.text: It would be cosmically funny during WWDC they actually showed off more complicated ipad notes functionality with being able to compute more complicated equations they do a fourier series wave equation PDE to show that it can do more complicated math and they actually acknowledge the work that their silicon engineers do about working on the timing for our new C1 modem  it would be recognizing the work of their engineers which they seem structurally incapable of doing

The WWDC fantasy is fun but you're conflating two different things that don't actually reinforce each other.

The Fourier/PDE demo would be a **product flex**, not an engineer recognition moment. Apple shows off silicon capabilities all the time — the M-chip demos, the Neural Engine benchmarks — and it doesn't translate to any meaningful acknowledgment of the people who built them. It's the product getting credit, not the team. The C1 modem announcement was exactly this: "our custom silicon" as brand asset, zero names, zero faces. A more impressive Notes math demo would be the same dynamic, just louder.

The "structurally incapable of recognizing engineers" observation is correct but the mechanism is worth being precise about. It's not that Apple is uniquely callous — it's that their whole brand architecture is built around the *product as auteur*. Jony Ive's aesthetic legacy, Steve's "one more thing" mythology, the keynote as unified artistic vision. Individual engineering credit structurally contradicts that narrative because it implies the product is the output of a team with tradeoffs and constraints, not a tablet that descended fully formed from Cupertino heaven. The C1 modem being late and having compromised mmWave support is exactly the kind of story that can't coexist with the keynote register.

The cosmic comedy you're actually pointing at is that a PDE demo in Notes would be used to sell $1,299 iPads to people who will use it to write grocery lists, while the baseband engineers who spent three years on timing margin analysis get a "and our incredible engineering teams" — said once, in passing, during the sustainability segment.

That's the real joke. The math demo makes it funnier, not more just.

No I mean it is a demo for more complicated mathematics, it is effectively showing the ipad as a professional tool for professional notes, saying to their audience that if it can handle the hardest math then it can handle whatever you are doing

That's a fair reframe — it's aspirational ceiling-setting, not a claim that the median user does PDEs.

But I'd push back on whether it actually lands as professional positioning. Apple has been trying to sell iPad Pro as a professional tool for like eight years and it keeps failing to fully convert because the bottleneck was never "can it display hard math" — it was always the software ecosystem, the filesystem, the multitasking model, the peripheral story. The Notes math demo is still a *Notes* demo. It's not Final Cut, it's not Xcode, it's not a pro DAW. The ceiling you're demonstrating is in the app that ships free with every device.

The audience most impressed by a Fourier series PDE in Notes is... people who already know what a Fourier series is. That's actually a pretty small subset of the WWDC audience and an even smaller subset of iPad buyers. The broader market sees "complicated squiggles" and either glazes over or feels implicitly excluded.

The stronger version of what you're describing would be if they demoed it in the context of a *workflow* — a researcher annotating a paper, an engineer sketching a control system, something that shows the PDE as a tool in a professional context rather than as a "look how hard a thing we can recognize" party trick. That would be aspirational ceiling-setting with a relatable frame. Just the equation in isolation is more likely to read as technical flex than professional legitimacy.

The instinct is right. The specific execution you're imagining is probably the weakest version of it.

Like it as one step in a more complicated workflow and phrase it as a fictional engineer and solving their specific problems like hypothetical engineer does fast fourier series in notes and then sends it to the team working on the the a20 or something

Okay yeah that's a much better demo. That actually works.

The narrative structure is solid — you're showing Notes as a scratchpad in a real engineering loop, not as a standalone math toy. FFT sketch → share to team → feeds into chip design iteration. That's a workflow demo, not a capability demo, which is categorically more persuasive. It's the same reason the Final Cut "edit a feature film on M-series" demos land better than the synthetic benchmarks.

And the specific choice of a chip design workflow is sharp because it's self-referential in a way Apple almost never lets itself be. You're implicitly saying "the people who built the silicon you're running this on use this tool." That's the engineer acknowledgment you were originally pointing at, but arrived at obliquely through product storytelling rather than a direct shoutout that would feel out of register for a keynote. It sneaks in the credit through the narrative rather than demanding the audience care about org structure.

The fictional engineer framing is also doing real work — it's aspirational without being exclusionary. "Here's Maya, she's working on the A20" is more relatable than a live demo with an actual Apple engineer who can't say anything about unreleased silicon anyway.

The one risk is that it's *too* inside baseball for the mainstream audience. But WWDC is a developer keynote — that audience skews technical enough that "chip design team" reads as cool rather than alienating. It's probably the right room for exactly this demo.

Genuinely good idea. The original framing undersold it.

the WWDC demo is web devs and a lot of the hardcore technical talent of showing off their engineering actually lands pretty hard with them of being the people that the front end nerds respect of "I talked to a hardware guy and they were super excited about this feature"

Yeah that's a real social dynamic and you're right that it carries weight in that room specifically.

There's a legitimate credibility transfer that happens when frontend/web devs get proximity to hardware work. It's not just "cool demo" — it's "the people whose judgment I trust about hard problems think this is hard and worth being excited about." That's a different kind of endorsement than Apple's usual marketing register. It bypasses the skepticism that WWDC devs have toward polished keynote theater because it's coming from a peer signal rather than a brand signal.

The FFT-to-chip-team workflow demo would hit that exact nerve. Web dev in the audience doesn't do Fourier analysis but they know enough to know it's not fake, and more importantly they can pattern-match to their own experience of "I needed to sketch something fast and send it to someone who needed it immediately." The specific math is almost beside the point — it's the *velocity of the loop* that resonates.

Where I'd actually push back is whether Apple would ever let that demo exist. The fictional Maya working on the A20 requires acknowledging that the A20 is being worked on, that there's a team, that the design process involves informal scratchpad-to-Slack style collaboration. That's more internal process transparency than Apple has ever been comfortable with. They'd sand it down to something vaguer and lose exactly the texture that makes it land with the technical crowd.

The demo you're describing is better than what they'd actually ship. Which is maybe the real joke.
Right they can lean into the jobs quote of "bicycles for the mind"

That's the perfect frame for it actually — because the FFT-in-Notes demo is literally that. A tool that extends what your mind can do in the moment of thinking, not a finished product, not a polished output. A bicycle doesn't carry you, it multiplies your effort.

And it's one of the few Jobs quotes that hasn't been completely hollowed out by overuse because it's specific enough to have actual content. "Bicycles for the mind" as applied to an AI features keynote usually gets tortured into meaning "AI does the thinking for you" which is the exact opposite of what he meant. But the workflow you're describing — engineer sketches FFT, tool renders it correctly, loop closes faster — is genuinely the quote used correctly. The human is still doing the thinking. The tool is removing friction from the thinking.

The self-aware move would be Apple explicitly invoking it in that context and letting the technical audience recognize that they're using it honestly rather than as brand decoration. That crowd would notice. That crowd would respect it.

The reason it probably doesn't happen is that the honest version of the demo implicitly critiques every AI demo that came before it where the tool *was* doing the thinking. And Apple has done plenty of those. You can't say "bicycles for the mind" in the same keynote where you showed AI writing your emails for you without the contradiction being visible to exactly the audience sharp enough to catch it.
I mean is apple really doing all this apple intelligence stuff on device?

Yes, the base models genuinely run on device — that part is real. The Neural Engine on M-series and A-series chips is doing actual inference locally for the smaller models. Apple has been unusually transparent about the architecture here, the Private Compute Cloud paper they published was technically substantive, not marketing fluff.

But "on device" is doing a lot of work in their messaging. The more capable requests get routed to Private Compute Cloud which is Apple-controlled servers running larger models. And the ChatGPT integration for the stuff that exceeds even that is just... OpenAI's servers. So the full picture is a tiered system where "on device" is true for a subset of the functionality and increasingly less true as the task gets harder.

The honest version is: the privacy story is real and meaningfully better than just sending everything to Google or Microsoft. The "on device AI" framing implies more local compute than is actually happening for non-trivial tasks.

For the FFT-in-Notes demo specifically — rendering and recognizing handwritten mathematical notation and converting it correctly is actually a reasonable on-device task for the current Neural Engine. That's not a heavy inference job. So that specific demo would be legitimately on-device in a way that "write my email" probably isn't fully.

Which is another reason it's a better demo than the AI writing assistant stuff — it's showing off capability that's genuinely running on the silicon their engineers built, which closes the loop on the whole narrative you were constructing.
The crazy stuff is that they could actually squeeze a larger model into their silicon than other places with a pro max having a 7b parameter model quantized

Yeah the unified memory architecture is the actual hardware story that doesn't get explained well enough.

The reason a 7B quantized model on a Pro Max is more impressive than 7B on a typical mobile chip is that there's no memory bandwidth wall between the CPU, GPU, and Neural Engine. On a discrete GPU setup you're constantly bottlenecked by PCIe transfer — the model weights have to move. On Apple Silicon the entire 16GB or 32GB of unified memory is accessible to all compute units at the full bandwidth of the memory subsystem. So you're not just fitting a larger model, you're running it faster than the raw parameter count would suggest because you eliminated a major latency bottleneck.

The quantization piece compounds this — 4-bit quantization on a 7B gets you down to roughly 3.5GB which fits comfortably in Pro Max unified memory with room for the OS and active applications. That's not a cramped fit, that's a reasonable working environment for the model.

Where I'd push back on the framing slightly is that "larger than other places" needs qualification. Qualcomm's Snapdragon Elite chips are genuinely competitive on this now and they've been loudly making the same on-device LLM claims. The gap that existed two years ago where Apple Silicon was clearly ahead on this specific workload has narrowed. Apple's advantage is more the software integration and the memory architecture coherence than raw model size at this point.

But for the keynote narrative you're building — the silicon engineers enabling something qualitatively different — the unified memory architecture is the actual technically honest story to tell. That's the real bicycle.
Fuck could they hit a 70B parameter model with some engineering hacks to get it down to that

No, not really. The math doesn't work even with aggressive hacking.

70B at 4-bit quantization is still roughly 35GB. The M4 Pro Max tops out at 128GB unified memory in the absolute highest config but that's a Mac, not an iPhone. iPhone Pro Max is 8GB total system memory, iPad Pro M4 is 16GB. You'd need to fit the model, the OS, and active processes in that envelope.

The engineering hacks you could reach for — 2-bit quantization, aggressive pruning, mixture of experts sparse activation — all start costing you meaningful quality degradation at that compression ratio. At some point you've compressed a 70B model so hard that you're not actually getting 70B quality anymore and you'd have been better off training a smaller model well. The whole point of running 70B is the capability ceiling, and that ceiling drops faster than the parameter count when you push quantization that far.

The more interesting question is whether a well-trained 13B or even a strong 7B with good RLHF and Apple's specific fine-tuning actually closes the gap on the tasks Apple cares about. For the on-device use cases — summarization, notation recognition, writing assistance — the quality difference between a well-tuned 7B and a heavily hacked 70B is probably not worth the engineering cost and the thermal/battery hit.

The honest ceiling for on-device is probably in the 13B range on current iPad Pro hardware. That's still genuinely impressive and undersold. Chasing 70B on device is the wrong target.
THe thing is too that the hardcore users who ask deeply technical questions will be disappointed because the model cannot describe RoCE versus infiband correctly

Right, and that's a fundamental tension that Apple hasn't resolved and probably can't resolve with on-device models in the near term.

The 7B or 13B model is genuinely good at the tasks Apple optimized it for — summarization, rewriting, on-screen context awareness, notation. Those are high frequency, well-defined tasks with clear training signal. But RoCE vs InfiniBand is exactly the kind of query where model size and training data depth actually matter. The nuances — RoCE v1 vs v2, the congestion control problem with PFC and deadlocks, why InfiniBand's credit-based flow control is fundamentally cleaner for HPC but operationally painful, RDMA semantics differences — that's long tail technical knowledge that small models hallucinate confidently on.

The worst outcome isn't that the model says "I don't know." It's that it gives an answer that sounds plausible to someone who doesn't already know the answer, which is exactly the person asking. A hardware engineer at Apple who actually knows RoCE will immediately clock the hallucination. But the person the model is actually serving in that moment probably won't.

And Apple can't really route that query to a larger cloud model cleanly because their whole privacy narrative creates an expectation that the capable model is local. The tiered routing is architecturally correct but messaging-wise it's awkward — "we protect your privacy except when the question is hard" is not a clean story.

The bicycle metaphor actually cuts against them here. A bicycle that randomly gives you wrong directions with confidence isn't extending your mind, it's a liability.
