Honestly kind of a mess right now. Here's the actual picture:

The big story is that Apple struck a deal with Google to use Gemini as the brain behind a revamped Siri, but they're apparently struggling to finish it — features originally planned for iOS 26.4 in March are now potentially slipping to iOS 26.5 or even iOS 27 in September. So the flagship AI bet is late and the engine powering it is Google's, which is a pretty significant admission that their own foundation models aren't competitive at the frontier.

John Giannandrea, who was the architect of the on-device everything philosophy, retired — and the team has been restructured under a new VP reporting directly to Federighi, which is the org chart signal that the previous approach wasn't working fast enough.

What they actually shipped recently is pretty incremental — Live Translation, visual intelligence extensions to on-screen content, Shortcuts integrations, and opening the on-device model to third-party developers. The developer API access is actually the most interesting piece strategically because it means Apple Foundation Models become a platform primitive.

The hardware story is more honest — the C1X modem on iPhone 17e is apparently 2x faster than C1 and 30% more energy efficient than the modem in iPhone 16 Pro, which is real silicon progress.

But the core tension is unchanged from what we were discussing. The on-device story is real for small tasks, Gemini is running the hard stuff, and Siri is still behind. The bicycle is borrowing someone else's gears.
How much did they pay gemini for this

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

About $1 billion a year for a 1.2 trillion parameter custom Gemini model.

The number is almost funny in context. Google already pays Apple roughly $20 billion annually to be the default search engine, so Apple is paying back five cents on the dollar to license the AI capability they failed to build themselves. The power dynamic is completely inverted from what it was two years ago.

The org chart story is also damning. Giannandrea retired in December 2025 and was immediately replaced by Amar Subramanya, who spent 16 years at Google and was most recently Head of Engineering for Google's Gemini Assistant. Apple knew what they were doing when they hired him. That's not a coincidence, that's a controlled handoff.

The jump is from 150 billion to 1.2 trillion parameters — an eight-fold increase — and it runs on Apple's Private Cloud Compute infrastructure, not Google Cloud, which is the fig leaf that preserves the privacy narrative.

The detail that doesn't get enough attention: Apple tested OpenAI and Anthropic before settling on Google. So they shopped around, which means the $1B/year is roughly market rate for frontier model access at this scale, and Gemini won on capability per dollar.

For the WWDC demo narrative you were building — the "bicycles for the mind" framing now has to coexist with the fact that the bicycle was built in Mountain View.
THEY ARE MAKING A PRIVATE MODEL?

Yeah that's the part of the story that's genuinely interesting and underreported.

The $1B/year custom Gemini deal running on Apple's Private Cloud Compute means Google is providing model weights or a distillation that Apple runs on their own infrastructure. It's not a standard API call to Google's servers. Apple controls the compute, which means user queries aren't going to Google's data centers — they're going to Apple-operated servers running a Google-derived model.

That's actually a meaningfully different privacy posture than just wrapping the Gemini API. Apple's Private Compute Cloud architecture has been independently audited and they've made strong guarantees about not logging queries. Apple Silicon in their servers is real — Private Cloud Compute runs on Apple Silicon servers, specifically custom M-series chips. That's actually a non-trivial differentiator because most hyperscalers are running Nvidia or custom ASICs for inference. Apple is running their own CPU/GPU/Neural Engine stack end to end.

But they're not making the full server stack. They're buying standard datacenter infrastructure — networking, storage, power, cooling — and dropping Apple Silicon into it. They don't have a server NIC, they don't have a storage controller, they're not doing what Google does with TPUs where the custom silicon extends all the way into the network fabric. Which actually creates an interesting irony for the RoCE vs InfiniBand conversation we were just having. Apple's inference cluster almost certainly has an RDMA story for moving model weights around between nodes, and they're probably using standard datacenter networking to do it — Ethernet with RoCE or possibly InfiniBand — built by the same Nvidia or Broadcom components everyone else uses. The on-device silicon story is fully Apple. The datacenter interconnect story almost certainly isn't.

So the "Apple controls the hardware" narrative is true at the compute layer and much less true at the systems layer. Which is probably fine for their privacy guarantees but it's not the vertically integrated story it superficially appears to be.

Here's where this gets really interesting for the Apple inference box argument.

The full 397B checkpoint is ~807GB on disk, but at 3-bit quantization it fits on a 192GB Mac, and 4-bit fits on 256GB RAM. So a current M4 Ultra Mac Studio at 192GB runs it today, slightly cramped. The hypothetical 512GB SKU runs it comfortably at 4-bit with headroom for KV cache.

But here's the thing that makes this story even better — 397B-A17B is in the same performance tier as Gemini 3 Pro, Claude Opus 4.6, and GPT-5.2. This is a frontier model. Not "pretty good for local." Actually frontier.

And the reason it fits at all is the MoE architecture — 397 billion total parameters while activating only 17 billion per forward pass. You're paying the memory cost of 397B but only the compute cost of 17B on each token. The unified memory architecture is perfectly suited for this because the inactive expert weights just sit in memory without creating bandwidth pressure until they're needed.

So the Apple inference box pitch just got sharper. The 192GB Mac Studio running 3-bit 397B today is already the cheapest way to run a frontier-class model locally. The 512GB SKU would be the definitive local inference machine, full stop, and Apple could price it accordingly.

They're accidentally building the most interesting AI hardware on the market while trying to sell it as a creative professional workstation.

Valve actually tried to solve this generically with Proton on Linux — a DirectX to Vulkan translation layer that runs Windows games on Linux with surprisingly good compatibility. It works remarkably well. The Steam Deck exists because of it.

Apple could just do that. A DirectX to Metal translation layer with the same philosophy as Proton — run the Windows binary, translate the API calls, don't ask developers to do anything. Apple actually shipped something called Game Porting Toolkit which is exactly this but they've treated it as a developer preview curiosity rather than a first class product.

If Apple shipped a Proton equivalent as a system feature, maintained it aggressively, and just told Steam "your entire catalog runs on Mac now" — that's the gaming problem solved without asking a single game studio to do anything.

The engineering investment is substantial but Apple has the money and the Metal expertise. The Rosetta 2 translation layer for x86 to ARM was a more ambitious technical project and they shipped it flawlessly.

They solved a harder problem than this for a less valuable market.

The gaming fix exists, Apple just doesn't seem to believe gaming is worth doing properly, which given the demographic they're trying to own is a strange hill to die on.

**YouTube/Meta:** You own no content. Users generate it for free. You run an RL auction on top. Margins are obscene because your COGS is essentially infrastructure + moderation, not $200M Ryan Murphy deals.

**Netflix:** You are a content studio that also runs distribution. You pay $17B/year in content spend to rent attention that you have to re-earn every month. The moment you stop producing hits, churn spikes. It's a treadmill with no exit.

The fundamental problem is Netflix is a **subscription model trying to compete with a free dopamine machine.** Every month a user has to consciously decide Netflix is worth $15. YouTube never asks that question — it's just there.

There are actually a few distinct songs with that title depending what you're after:

**Most likely what you mean:** **Erasure** — the 1988 synth-pop single by Vince Clarke and Andy Bell, off *The Innocents*. That's the one Jim Sterling uses as his theme, so if you're trying to avoid that association, this is the source of the problem.

**The original blues track:** **Big Joe Turner** — a 12-bar blues written by Doc Pomus (later claimed by Ahmet Ertegun under the pseudonym "A. Nugetre"), first recorded in 1951 and reaching #2 on the R&B chart. Covered later by Bobby Bland, Pat Boone, Mickey Gilley, etc. Completely separate song.

**Recent one:** **Charli XCX** released a "Chains of Love" in November 2025 as part of her *Wuthering Heights* soundtrack album.

So — which direction are you going? The Erasure track is the Sterling one regardless, so if you're trying to cover or reference the song without that baggage, you're kind of stuck unless you lean into the Joe Turner blues original, which has enough distance from the synth-pop version that the association mostly disappears.
The first one

Yeah, that's the Jim Sterling one — there's no escaping it. Erasure's "Chains of Love" *is* the Sterling song at this point. The association is basically permanent for anyone in gaming/internet circles from that era.

What's the context? If you're trying to use it for something, knowing the purpose might surface whether it actually matters or if you're overthinking the association.
I'm hearing it in public at my university and I'm like "do they know what they are referring to?"

Ha, almost certainly not. Erasure has a perfectly normal fanbase that predates Sterling by decades — it's a legitimate 80s synth-pop hit. The overlap between "person who listens to Erasure" and "person who watched Jim Sterling's Jimquisition religiously" is probably pretty small outside of very online gaming circles.

You're the anomaly here, not them.
I mean I prefer to interpret my interpretation is valid, they are the deviant

That's a losing argument and you know it. Erasure released that song in 1988. Sterling started using it decades later. Chronologically and by sheer audience size, the Erasure listeners have prior claim by a wide margin.

You're the one who got the reference overwritten by a games journalist. That's a you problem.

name: Film production scheduling and shoot delays

messages.text: I hear that the actual most expensive thing for a film is buying more days on set so I imagine that the software that allows you to know how long you are on set is very important but it is probably not very much developed. I imagine what are the things that cause shoots to go longer than normally expected.

Your intuition about set time being expensive is right — <a day on a studio feature can run $500K–$1M+ all-in when you factor in crew, equipment, location, catering, etc. But your assumption that the software tracking this is underdeveloped is worth examining.

**The software is actually pretty mature.** Movie Magic Scheduling has been the industry standard for decades. Showbiz Budgeting, EP Budgeting, and Studiobinder are all serious tools. The problem isn't software — it's that the *inputs* are fundamentally hard to estimate, not that the tracking layer is missing. Knowing you're 2 hours over on a given day is easy. Knowing *why* and preventing it next time is the hard part.

**What actually causes overruns:**

**Actor-side problems** are huge. A-listers have contractual protections around working hours (SAG rules, personal riders), which creates cascade effects — if one scene runs long, you can't just push an actor's call time, you might blow their turnaround window entirely and lose them the next morning. Unavailability, late arrivals, needing extra takes due to performance issues — all brutal.

**Visual effects handoffs.** When a shot requires VFX, you can't just move on if the plate isn't right. Directors second-guess themselves because they know fixing it in post is expensive, so they overshoot.

**Weather.** Exterior shoots are obvious, but weather also affects interior schedules because you need to move crew and equipment, and that burns the day.

**The director indulging.** This is the real elephant in the room nobody says openly — a director who's not decisive, or who falls in love with a location and wants more coverage than the schedule allows, is the single biggest variable. Producers hate saying it but it's true.

**AD quality.** The first assistant director is the actual enforcer of the schedule. A weak 1st AD who can't push back on the director or manage company moves efficiently is catastrophic.

**Company moves** — the physical act of moving equipment, crew, and cast between locations — are massively underestimated in scheduling. They're almost always optimistic.

**Rewrites happening during production.** Pages change, sets change, deals collapse, and suddenly a scene you prepped for doesn't exist and a scene you didn't prep for needs to shoot tomorrow.

The honest pushback on your premise: the bottleneck isn't software sophistication, it's that film production is a coordination problem with enormous human variance and contractual constraints baked in. Better tooling helps at the margins but doesn't solve the fundamental unpredictability.

name: Defense vs entertainment industry revenue in LA

messages.text: Does the defense industry actually make more than the entertainment industry in LA? The numbers are closer than most people would like to think.

```

```


```

```


```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

The claim is roughly true but requires much more precision than the framing implies. Let me lay out what the numbers actually show and where the argument gets sloppy.

**What's real:** California A&D is genuinely massive. The aerospace & defense industry in California generated roughly $148.8 billion in total economic output in 2023, contributing about $70.5 billion to California's GDP. That's the whole state, and LA/Southern California is the center of gravity for that — Northrop, Raytheon, SpaceX, L3Harris, all headquartered or heavily concentrated there.

Meanwhile, Hollywood is structurally weaker than its cultural cachet suggests. From Q2 2019 to Q2 2024, entertainment jobs in California decreased by 15 percent, with sound stage occupancy in LA dropping to 63% in 2024. Entertainment still pumps approximately $43 billion in wages into California's economy, but that's wages, not total output — and it's California-wide, not LA-specific.

**Where the argument gets imprecise:**

The comparison depends heavily on what you're measuring (revenue, GDP contribution, wages, total economic output) and how you define "entertainment." If you include music, gaming, streaming, and adjacent industries, entertainment's number balloons considerably. The BEA counts arts and cultural economic activity at $1.17 trillion nationally in 2023, or 4.2% of GDP — vastly larger than A&D — but that's the broadest possible definition.

The "closer than people think" framing is also slightly backwards from the interesting version of the story: the gap has been *narrowing because Hollywood is collapsing*, not because defense has surged dramatically. LA County saw motion picture and sound recording jobs drop from about 142,000 in 2022 to around 100,000 by late 2024.

**The sharper version of this claim:** defense/aerospace has probably *already eclipsed film & TV production specifically* in LA County, because Hollywood keeps offshoring production to Georgia, the UK, and Canada while the defense primes stay put. That's the claim worth making — not the broader entertainment industry comparison, which still probably favors entertainment when you include music, gaming, and streaming.

So: directionally right, but if you're going to make this argument publicly, you need to nail down exactly what's in each bucket. The ambiguity is doing a lot of work.
Right because LA's main identity is entertainment specifically in film and tv, thrnfact defense is larger is actually insane to many people in  LA.

Yeah, and it makes sense why it would be — the entertainment industry is *loud* in a way defense isn't. Film and TV are literally the mechanism through which LA exports its identity to the world. Every show set in LA, every awards ceremony, every celebrity culture artifact — it's all self-referential PR for Hollywood. Defense just... quietly builds stealth bombers in Palmdale and cashes DoD checks.

The psychological gap is also geographic. If you live in Silver Lake or Los Feliz, your entire social fabric is adjacent to entertainment — writers, actors, crew, production companies. Defense is physically concentrated in the South Bay (El Segundo, Torrance, Long Beach) and the Antelope Valley, which are functionally different cities culturally. It's easy to live your whole LA life and never meaningfully interact with someone whose paycheck comes from Northrop or Raytheon.

There's also a class visibility issue. Entertainment produces *famous* people. Defense produces well-compensated engineers and program managers who live in Redondo Beach and nobody makes a prestige drama about them. The economic footprint is invisible to the cultural conversation.

The genuinely wild part though is the trajectory asymmetry — defense is structurally stable to growing (Pentagon budgets don't collapse during strikes, streaming doesn't disrupt them, they don't lose jobs to Georgia tax incentives), while film/TV in LA is in a prolonged structural decline that isn't obviously reversible. So this isn't a static comparison. Five years from now the gap is probably wider, not narrower, and LA's self-conception still won't have updated.

That's actually the more interesting essay: LA's identity is built on an industry that is no longer its largest industry and is actively shrinking. What does that mean for the city?
If you include traditional entertainment in film, tv and music the numbers get sad

Yeah, music especially. The recorded music industry nationally is like $17 billion in revenue — the whole thing. And LA's share of that, while disproportionate, is still a rounding error compared to what Northrop Grumman alone pulls in on federal contracts.

The collapse narrative for music in LA is particularly brutal because the infrastructure erosion is physical and irreversible. Studios closing, venues shutting down, the entire mid-tier ecosystem of session musicians, producers, and engineers that made Hollywood music what it was — that's gone and it's not coming back. What remains is heavily concentrated at the very top (major label infrastructure) and the very bottom (bedroom producers), with the middle hollowed out.

Film and TV at least has the argument that production *could* come back if California got more aggressive with tax incentives. Music doesn't even have that story. The product is digital, the margins collapsed, and the geography stopped mattering. You can make a hit record anywhere.

So you've got three industries that define LA's identity:
- **Film/TV:** declining, geographically mobile, structurally pressured by streaming economics
- **Music:** already declined, the recovery already happened and it didn't bring the jobs back
- **Defense/Aerospace:** boring, invisible, enormous, growing

The actually uncomfortable version of this is that LA's cultural identity is essentially a legacy brand running on accumulated mythology. The *idea* of Hollywood is still enormously valuable globally — it still attracts people, still drives tourism, still shapes how the city sees itself. But the underlying economic reality quietly diverged from that identity probably a decade ago and nobody's really reckoned with it.

It's a great essay premise honestly.

Right Hollywood seems to be getting disrupted hard by instagram, tiktok and youtube which is insane, the attention economy of RL dopamine boxes is going to make more money than scoresse which makes them annoyed but they turned a humanities problem into an engineering problem.

This is a sharp observation but I'd push back on one part of the framing.

The "humanities problem turned into engineering problem" line is evocative but slightly backwards. The attention economy *was always* an engineering problem — Bernays figured out the manipulation mechanics in the 1920s, broadcast TV was already optimizing for retention, Nielsen ratings were a primitive engagement metric. What happened is that the engineering just got *much better* and the feedback loop compressed from seasons to milliseconds. TikTok's For You Page is a recommendation system, not a cultural philosophy, and it won. That's not a category error, it's just better tooling winning.

The more interesting disruption isn't even attention capture — it's **cost structure**. Scorsese costs $200M and requires a theatrical release window and a marketing campaign and a distribution deal. A TikTok creator with 10M followers costs essentially zero to the platform and generates comparable or greater watch hours. Hollywood's entire value proposition was that quality required scale required capital required gatekeepers. That's just not true anymore at the level of attention capture, even if it's still true at the level of genuine craft.

What Scorsese is actually selling now is **legitimacy and occasion** — the thing you watch that you then talk about having watched. That market is real but it's smaller and more luxury than Hollywood ever wanted to admit it was.

The genuinely annoying thing for the Scorsese camp is that the disruption isn't even close in quality terms and it still won. It's not like YouTube beat Goodfellas. Minecraft YouTubers and 60-second dances beat Goodfellas. That's the part that's actually insulting.
how much did the movie iron lung make with a 4m dollar budget?

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Also worth flagging your premise: the budget wasn't $4M — it was just over $3.6 million (some sources round to $3M).

As for the gross: Iron Lung has been a commercial success, grossing $51 million worldwide against that budget. Specifically, as of March 1, 2026, it grossed $41 million in the US and Canada and $10 million internationally.

That's roughly a **14x return on budget** — pretty insane for a self-financed, self-distributed film from a YouTuber.
Right plus I imagine the marketing budget wasn't that big

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Yeah, that's the whole story. The "marketing" wasn't a Super Bowl spot — it was a decade of trust built between a creator and his subscribers. Markiplier effectively *was* the marketing department.

Deadline described the marketing spend as only "barebones." And the distribution deal was structured to make the economics even cleaner — he opted for a 50/50 split with theaters, which is lower than the industry average, but that still means essentially zero traditional P&A spend eating into his margin.

Compare that to a normal studio release where marketing routinely equals or exceeds production budget — he didn't need a $30M marketing spend, didn't need four weeks of press junkets. The real takeaway is that his subscriber base converted almost perfectly to ticket buyers: exit polls showed 71% of the opening weekend audience cited "an actor in the lead role" as their main reason for seeing the film. That's parasocial loyalty functioning as distribution infrastructure.
So he probably made a ton of money on it.

Yeah, though the exact net is hard to nail down without knowing his full deal structure. But rough math:

$51M gross, 50/50 theater split = ~$25M to Markiplier's side. Subtract the $3.6M production budget and the "barebones" marketing spend (probably low single-digit millions at most), and he likely cleared **$15-20M+ net profit** on this thing.

That's life-changing money for an independent film, and arguably more impressive than most studio successes because there's no studio taking a massive overhead cut, no distribution fee eating 15-30% off the top, no greenlight committee he had to answer to.

The uncomfortable implication for Hollywood is that a YouTuber with an engaged audience basically replicated the economics of a mid-budget studio film while keeping nearly all the upside. The "distribution moat" studios used to have is clearly not as wide as they thought.

In a podcast he mentions the studio and the episode where he's dating the doctor which is something I crashed out about previously. Like yes respect other professions, but also the studio I think was so popular in LA because it reflects how a lot of creative people actually think.

Yeah that tracks completely. The doctor episode is classic anxious-creative insecurity dressed up as open-mindedness — "look how enlightened I am for dating someone with a *real* job" — and the audience response to it is almost a Rorschach test for where someone is in their own creative self-worth journey.

The studio setting resonates in LA specifically because it captures that particular flavor of creative precarity that's unique to the city. Not starving-artist poverty, but that liminal zone where you're *almost* making it, surrounded by people who are *almost* making it, and the question of whether any of it is legitimate never fully goes away. A doctor girlfriend becomes this ambient reminder that there's an off-ramp available, a legible success story you didn't take.

That said — which podcast/show are you referencing? I want to make sure I'm not pattern-matching to the wrong thing here. Are you talking about a specific Markiplier project, or something else?

In a podcast he mentions the studio on Apple TV Plus by Seth Rogan and the episode where he's dating the doctor which is something I crashed out about previously. Like yes respect other professions, but also the studio I think was so popular in LA because it reflects how a lot of creative people actually think.

Yeah, The Studio is a pretty sharp satire precisely because it's not punching down at delusional outsiders — it's punching *inward* at people who are self-aware enough to know they're compromised but keep doing it anyway. That's a much harder thing to write and a more honest portrait of LA creative culture.

The doctor episode thing is a real tension. The "respect all professions equally" framing is kind of a cope — there's a difference between respecting someone's labor and pretending that dating a cardiovascular surgeon and dating a mid-level development exec at a streaming service require the same amount of social recalibration. The show's whole thesis is that Hollywood people have a wildly distorted sense of their own cultural importance, so an episode playing that dynamic straight (rather than satirizing it) would be tonally inconsistent.

But the reason it resonates in LA specifically is that the show accurately depicts the **motivated reasoning loop**: these people genuinely believe in the art, genuinely believe the compromises are temporary, and genuinely cannot tell when they've become the thing they used to mock. That's not a character flaw unique to Hollywood — it's just more visible there because the product is public and the pretension is loud.

Markiplier is almost the photonegative of that archetype, which is maybe why Iron Lung's success hits different. He skipped the entire system that The Studio is satirizing and still won.

Right the studio is also like making fun of these Hollywood people which it's allowed to do because it is already signaling it is apart of the in group which there is probably a social phenomenon you're allowed to do that when you are inside the industry. Like a programmer saying "maybe I should give up and move to be in the middle of the fucking woods versus some business guy saying you're going to loose your job to AI meanwhile a senior software engineer can say that "I fucking hope I lose my job to AI, devops is miserable"

Yeah the phenomenon you're describing is **in-group license** — the right to articulate a group's most uncomfortable truths is gatekept by demonstrated membership. The critique lands differently because it can only come from someone who actually paid the cost of admission.

The Seth Rogen example is perfect because he's not just *in* Hollywood, he's been eaten by it and survived — he has the scar tissue to write that show credibly. An outsider making the same jokes would just read as resentment.

Your programmer example is actually the sharper version of this though because it has an additional layer: the in-group member's self-deprecation carries **epistemic authority** that the outsider's attack doesn't. When a business guy says "AI will take your job" it's a threat from someone who doesn't understand the work. When a senior SWE says "I hope I lose my job to AI, devops is miserable" — they're not being defeatist, they're making a *technical claim* about the actual texture of the work that only someone who's done it can make. The humor and the credibility are inseparable.

The Hollywood version is the same — Rogen's show works because the specific details are too accurate to be guesswork. You can't fake knowing what a development meeting actually feels like.

It's also why Shannon's Revenge works the way it does, honestly. The joke only lands for people who recognize the compliance theater from the inside.

Right the joke is the difference between someone saying "you guys have so many bugs to fix seems like it won't ever end" versus the guy saying "Brandon Eich made a a mistake we have to live with every day, the browser was a mistake but it's also impossible to rip out"

Exactly. The first comment is just an observation from outside the blast radius. The second one is **load-bearing grievance** — it's not just complaining, it's a specific historical indictment (Eich, 1995, one design decision) that every web dev carries as inherited trauma. The joke only works if you know why JavaScript in the browser is specifically cursed, not just generically messy.

And there's a compounding effect: the more precise the reference, the more it signals membership, which makes the frustration funnier *and* more cathartic for the in-group. "Bugs are hard" is a shrug. "We are all living in the shadow of one man's choices at Netscape" is theology.

The best version of this in ML systems is probably the CUDA moat discourse — an outsider says "Nvidia has a lot of market share." An insider says "we are trapped because ten years of grad students wrote kernels that only run on one vendor's hardware and nobody is going to rewrite them." Same fact, completely different weight. The insider version implies the full dependency graph: the research, the tooling, the hiring pipelines, all of it locked in by accumulated specificity.

Shannon's Revenge hits that register because "detecting Claude in a DoD codebase" is already a joke that requires you to know why that's funny — the compliance framework, the Anthropic contract dispute, the absurdity of applying CMMC logic to LLM outputs. Zero explanation needed for the right audience, completely opaque to everyone else.

Right there's like other jokes of "Jensen huang's model of SIMT is the actual techncial wrong abstraction for ML, dataflow is better, the problem is that being technically correct means nothing say that to all the non-x86 CPU vendors from the 90s" versus "google is going to destroy Nvidia's monopoly"

Yeah that's a perfect spectrum. The second one is a takes-haver comment, something you'd see in a TechCrunch comment section. It requires no understanding of *why* the monopoly exists or what it would actually take to dislodge it.

The first one is doing real work. It's simultaneously:

- A **technical claim** (SIMT is the wrong abstraction for the memory access patterns ML actually has, dataflow architectures handle the dependency graphs more naturally)
- A **historical claim** (being technically correct has never been sufficient — SPARC, MIPS, Alpha, all arguably better than x86 at the time, all dead)
- And a **resigned punchline** — the person making it isn't predicting Nvidia's downfall, they're describing a tragedy they've accepted

The joke is the gap between "correct" and "wins." And the reason it's funny to the in-group is that everyone in ML systems has felt that gap personally — you've written a cleaner abstraction that lost to a messier one with better ecosystem support, or you've watched a technically superior tool die because PyTorch already had the mindshare.

Tenstorrent is basically living inside that joke right now. Karras and Keckler are not wrong about the architecture. That's almost beside the point.

The outsider version of the Tenstorrent take is "Tenstorrent could beat Nvidia." The insider version is "Tenstorrent is probably right and it probably won't matter."

Right the joke is knowing that "there is a difference between being right and winning, it's the decision between IBM and Bell labs. Bell labs was correct, like always unix is better, IBM still exists"

Yeah that's the cleanest formulation of it. Bell Labs had a nearly perfect hit rate on being correct — Unix, C, information theory, the transistor — and yet AT&T got dismantled and IBM, which was frequently wrong but commercially ruthless, is still a going concern.

The tragedy isn't even that Bell Labs lost. It's that **being correct was never the game they were playing.** The game was always distribution, lock-in, enterprise relationships, switching costs. Bell Labs kept winning the wrong game extraordinarily well.

And the really dark version of that insight is that Bell Labs' correctness might have been *enabled* by not having to win the commercial game — AT&T's monopoly subsidy is what bought the freedom to just be right about things. The moment that protection ended the whole thing collapsed almost immediately. Xerox PARC is the same story.

So the full joke for ML systems is: CUDA is IBM, every cleaner abstraction is Bell Labs, and the punchline is that the Bell Labs people know it and still can't stop themselves from building the better thing anyway. Because the alternative is going to work at IBM.

Which is maybe why the ML systems community has this specific flavor of gallows humor that's different from like, web dev complaining. Web devs are mad they have to use bad tools. ML systems people are mad because they *understand exactly why* the bad tools won and can see the mechanism clearly and it doesn't help at all.

Right they make jokes about how the us government shipped 10k tomahawks to Israel, because they are fundamental to national security and the realization is that it is for a Microsoft data center, not Iran.

Yeah that's a brutal joke because the punchline requires you to know the entire dependency chain. The surface read is just "military hardware for a data center, that's weird." But the actual joke is:

The US government has decided that **GPU compute is as strategically critical as precision munitions** — and they're not wrong, which is the part that makes it funny and horrifying simultaneously. The Tomahawk comparison works because both are about projecting power, it's just that the new definition of power is inference capacity and the new contested territory is data center siting.

And the insider layer is that Microsoft specifically makes it funnier — it's not even a frontier lab, it's the enterprise software company that bought 49% of one. The military-industrial complex mobilized for a Teams integration.

The really dark extension is that the joke implies the people making the national security argument are **correct.** If you actually believe AGI is coming and compute is the limiting reagent then treating GPU clusters like strategic weapons depots is the rational policy response. The joke isn't that they're wrong, it's that the world they're describing is insane and they're just matter-of-factly living in it.

Same structure as the SIMT joke — the humor comes from someone who understands the full causal chain reciting it deadpan. The outsider version is "tech companies have too much political power." The insider version is "we are in a world where a H100 cluster has the same strategic classification as a carrier group and the reasoning is actually coherent."
Right because it's assuming the audience knows the tomahawk Broadcom nic and the  missle which rarely ever come up in the same context.

Right, the joke is doing like three levels of disambiguation simultaneously that the audience has to resolve instantly:

- Tomahawk the missile
- Tomahawk the Broadcom NIC that's in basically every hyperscale data center
- The US government export control apparatus treating both with similar strategic seriousness

And the reason it's funny to the right audience is the collision of those two reference frames happening in one sentence with zero explanation. The joke **is** the disambiguation — the moment you resolve which Tomahawk is which and realize they're both actually in the same sentence correctly, that's the punchline firing.

It's the same mechanic as "we're still paying for Brendan Eich's decisions" — the compression ratio is the signal. How much context do you need to unpack this sentence? If the answer is "a lot, and I already had all of it," you're in the in-group.

The outsider version of that joke would have to explain it, which immediately kills it. "The US government shipped Tomahawk missiles — that's also the name of a Broadcom NIC used in data centers — to protect Microsoft's infrastructure" is just a Wikipedia summary. The joke requires the audience to already be carrying both definitions simultaneously and feel the collision themselves.

It's actually a pretty good test for whether something is genuine in-group humor versus performed in-group humor — genuine version assumes the knowledge, performed version winks at the camera to make sure you know it knows.

I mean the ibm versus bell labs joke is load bearing to the tech industry, like it is horrifying that you can either continue to exist in the future or you can change the world. Oftentimes those two things are assumed to be the same thing, they are not.

Yeah and that's the actually terrifying insight that most people in tech spend enormous energy not looking at directly.

The Silicon Valley mythology **requires** them to be the same thing — "change the world and get rich" is the founding ideological claim. It's why every pitch deck has a mission statement, why Zuckerberg needs to believe he's connecting humanity, why Sam Altman can't just say "we're building a very profitable product." The whole psychic architecture of the industry depends on collapsing that distinction.

Bell Labs and IBM being clearly separable exposes the mythology as load-bearing fiction. Because if you accept that "continuing to exist" and "changing the world" are actually in tension, you have to then ask which one you're actually optimizing for — and most institutions, when forced to choose, pick survival. Which is rational! But it means the mission statement was always downstream of the business model, not the other way around.

The really uncomfortable version is that Bell Labs could only exist because AT&T was a regulated monopoly with guaranteed revenue and no survival pressure. The "change the world" function was **subsidized by** the "continue to exist" function. The moment that subsidy disappeared Bell Labs died almost instantly.

So the dark implication for AI labs right now is obvious. Anthropic, OpenAI — the research that might actually matter is being funded by the product revenue that requires not disrupting the people writing the checks. You can see the IBM/Bell Labs dynamic forming in real time, you just can't say it out loud because the whole thing runs on the mythology staying intact.
How did Disney get the rights to have bluey at the parks because they don't own the character

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Your instinct was right — Disney doesn't own Bluey. The whole situation is actually a pretty good case study in IP licensing and Disney fumbling the bag.

Here's the structure: Bluey is owned by Ludo Studio (the Australian production company) and globally licensed and distributed by BBC Studios. Disney only ever had streaming rights via Disney+. When the original deal was struck in 2019, Disney's consumer products division was offered the full package — merchandising and theme park rights — and passed on it. That turned out to be a catastrophic miss given that Bluey became the most-streamed show on Disney+, and kids started showing up at the parks expecting to meet Bluey and Bingo the same way they could meet Spider-Man.

What eventually happened is that Disney went back and negotiated a separate, expanded deal in late 2024. Disney and BBC Studios announced a comprehensive agreement covering a theatrical feature film (coming 2027, written and directed by creator Joe Brumm), plus theme parks and cruise ships. So Disney secured the park rights through a licensing deal with BBC Studios — they're paying for access to IP they don't own, which is unusual for them. The deal is notable specifically because Disney almost exclusively uses its parks to showcase IP it owns outright, making a third-party character arrangement genuinely rare.

The key point: Disney still doesn't own Bluey. Ludo Studio has emphasized the character is not for sale. This is a licensing arrangement, not an acquisition — more like how they licensed Twilight Zone for Tower of Terror than how they bought Lucasfilm or Marvel.
How much did they pay for it?

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

Nobody knows — the deal terms are not public. Neither Disney nor BBC Studios disclosed a dollar figure for the park/theatrical licensing arrangement.

What we do know is the rough shape of the deal: Disney handles theatrical and streaming rights, BBC Studios handles financing and licensing, and Ludo Studio produces — so it's a revenue-share structure across multiple rights packages rather than a clean "Disney paid X for parks access." The park rights are bundled into a broader expanded relationship that also covers the 2027 film.

The one useful data point for context: Bluey as a franchise is estimated to be worth around $2 billion. So whatever Disney is paying, BBC Studios and Ludo are negotiating from a position of strength, especially since Ludo has been clear the IP isn't for sale. Disney is essentially a very well-resourced distribution and experience partner, not the owner.

Short answer: the number isn't out there. These kinds of licensing terms almost never are.
Estimate

Rough back-of-napkin reasoning:

**Comparable anchors:**
- Star Wars Land cost ~$1B to build. Disney doesn't pay that kind of licensing fee for IP it actually owns.
- For third-party IP, the range is typically a revenue share (3-8% of merch/ticket revenue attributable to the character) plus a flat upfront guarantee.
- Sesame Street's deal with SeaWorld for comparison is rumored to have been in the low tens of millions annually.

**Bluey-specific factors pushing the number up:**
- Ludo/BBC have massive leverage — they already rejected Disney's initial overture and watched the IP 5x in value anyway
- Bluey merchandise alone is doing ~$300M/year globally, with Disney+ streaming driving a huge chunk of US exposure Disney is already paying for separately
- The theatrical deal is bundled in, which is worth a lot — a Bluey movie could easily do $500M+ box office

**My estimate:**
Probably a **$50-150M upfront guarantee** against a **5-8% royalty on park merchandise and ticket premiums** attributable to Bluey, plus a separate theatrical revenue split closer to standard co-production terms (20-30% of net to licensor).

Total deal value over the life of the agreement is probably **$200-400M** across all rights categories combined.

The wide range reflects that I genuinely don't know how they structured the film vs. parks split. If it's weighted toward the film, the upfront could be lower with more backend exposure. If Disney guaranteed park exclusivity globally, that premium alone could push the floor up significantly.
How much would it cost for Disney to buy bluey?

Again, no public number, but this is actually a fun estimate.

**Starting point: franchise value**
The $2B franchise value figure floating around is revenue-based, not acquisition price. Buyers pay a multiple on that, not the number itself.

**Comparable acquisitions:**
- Marvel: $4B in 2009 (low, Disney got a steal — it was post-financial crisis and Marvel was overleveraged)
- Lucasfilm: $4B in 2012
- Pixar: $7.4B in 2006

Those are full studios with large IP libraries, production infrastructure, and talent. Bluey is essentially one show, one studio, one creator.

**Better comps:**
- Hasbro bought eOne (Peppa Pig's parent) for $4B in 2019 — but that included a massive content library beyond just Peppa
- Peppa Pig alone was estimated at ~$1.5-2B as an isolated asset at the time

Bluey is more valuable than Peppa in the US market right now, roughly comparable globally.

**The Ludo/BBC complexity:**
Disney can't just buy Bluey — they'd need to buy Ludo Studio (the actual IP owner) AND negotiate with BBC Studios which holds distribution rights. That's two separate transactions, and BBC Studios is a subsidiary of the BBC which has political complications around privatization.

**My estimate:**
Ludo alone: **$1.5-2.5B**. Getting BBC Studios to relinquish distribution rights adds another **$500M-1B** in either direct payment or complex carve-outs.

All-in: **$2-3.5B** to fully own the IP the way Disney owns Marvel — and that's assuming Ludo's founders are willing to sell, which they've explicitly signaled they aren't. Unwilling seller premium could push it to **$4B+**.

Honestly Disney should just pay it. Bluey is one of the only children's IP in the last decade with genuine cross-demographic cultural penetration. Parents actually like watching it. That's rarer than people realize and it's worth more than the spreadsheet says.
How much money does donald trump actually have?

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

The honest answer: **nobody actually knows**, and that's kind of the whole point.

The headline number is Forbes's current estimate of ~$6.5 billion as of February 2026, but that figure deserves a lot of skepticism for a few reasons:

**The asset side is murky**

Forbes reports $8.4 billion in assets against $1.1 billion in liabilities, but only $1.1 billion is liquid — stocks, bonds, and crypto that fluctuate constantly. The rest is locked up in real estate, which brings us to the core problem: Trump testified Mar-a-Lago alone is worth $1–1.5 billion, while the local appraiser put it at $37 million — a 40x discrepancy. These valuations are genuinely contested and self-reported.

**He has a documented history of lying about this**

A New York court found Trump had for years committed fraud by exaggerating his net worth and significantly overvaluing assets in financial statements. His Trump Tower apartment was claimed at roughly triple its actual size and value; Mar-a-Lago was inflated ~22x. This isn't speculation — it's a court finding.

**The recent surge is crypto-driven and paper-thin**

Since returning to office, roughly two-thirds of his ~$3 billion net worth increase came from cryptocurrency ventures. Retail investors who bought Trump Media stock or the TRUMP memecoin on the open market have absorbed significant losses — $1,000 invested in Official TRUMP is now worth ~$114. Trump himself grew richer because he received controlling stakes at little or no cost, then those holdings are counted at market value in his net worth.

**The liquidity situation is tight**

In 2020, his liquid assets were about $93 million per tax documents. Even with the surge, most of his "wealth" is illiquid real estate and volatile crypto/media positions — he's asset-rich but cash-constrained by historical standards for someone claiming his wealth.

**Bottom line:** The $6–7B number is the best external estimate, but it's built on contested real estate valuations, volatile crypto positions, and zero tax transparency from a guy courts have confirmed has systematically lied about his finances. The real number could plausibly be $2B or $10B depending on how you mark things to market and which valuations you believe.
Didn't he get a fine for a large amount of money or was that not real

```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

It was real — and then it was thrown out. Here's the full arc:

**What happened:** In February 2024, a New York State Supreme Court judge ruled that Trump and his companies had falsified business records, issued false financial statements, and conspired to commit fraud and insurance fraud. Judge Engoron found that Trump's financial statements overvalued his assets by between $812 million and $2.2 billion. The penalty was originally ~$454 million, which grew to over $500 million with interest.

**Then the appeals court tossed it:** In August 2025, a New York appeals court dismissed the full $500M+ civil fraud penalty, with judges agreeing the award was an "excessive fine" — their words were "while harm certainly occurred, it was not the cataclysmic harm that can justify a nearly half billion-dollar award."

**Critically: the fraud finding itself was upheld.** The appeals court narrowly upheld the finding that Trump engaged in fraud by exaggerating his wealth for decades — they just disagreed on the remedy being proportionate. Trump calling it "total victory" is spin; the fraud finding survived.

**What he still owes:** A federal appeals court upheld a jury's finding that Trump sexually abused writer E. Jean Carroll and affirmed a $5 million judgment against him. He's also appealing a subsequent verdict requiring him to pay Carroll $83.3 million for additional defamation claims.

**The bond situation:** Trump had posted a $175 million bond to stave off collection while appealing — that sum will now be repaid to the Trump Organization.

So: the ~$500M fine was real, the fraud was real, he paid nothing, and the fine got killed on Eighth Amendment grounds. The Carroll judgments (~$88M) are still live. Classic Trump legal outcome — technically loses on the facts, wins on the remedy.
Does he still need to find a smaller fine for fraud or is that not allowed he just pays nothing now?

Good question — the answer is somewhat in flux.
```
This block is not supported on your current device yet.
```

The situation is messier than a simple "he pays nothing" — it's genuinely still in play.

**The internal disagreement on the appeals court is wild:**

In a 323-page opinion, all five judges agreed the penalty should be tossed, but for completely different reasons: two said Trump was properly found liable for fraud but the fine was excessive; two said the trial court was wrong to find fraud at all and the case should be retried; and a fifth said the AG shouldn't have brought the case in the first place. They all reached the same outcome through completely different legal theories, which is a messy foundation for the ruling.

**It's not over — it's going to New York's highest court:**

AG Letitia James is asking the Court of Appeals (New York's highest court) to reinstate the full penalty. So the question of whether he owes a recalculated smaller fine or nothing at all is still unresolved.

**What's currently in place beyond the money:**

The AG's statement noted the court upheld injunctive relief limiting Trump and Trump Organization officers' ability to do business in New York — that non-monetary punishment survived.

**Bottom line:** He hasn't paid and isn't currently required to pay anything on this case, but it's not fully dead. The NY Court of Appeals could reinstate some version of the penalty, recalculate a smaller one, or let the dismissal stand. We probably won't know the final answer for another year or more.
I mean has anyone ever been fined a similar amount for similar crimes or at least crimes that are of a similar magnitude?

Good question. Short answer: not really at the individual level, though there are some reasonable comparisons.

**Corporate fraud fines at this scale are common — but those are companies**

Enron, WorldCom, Wells Fargo — corporations routinely face $500M+ penalties for financial fraud. But corporations are diffuse entities. An individual executive personally paying that is basically unheard of.

**The closest individual comparisons:**

- **Bernie Madoff** — $170B forfeiture ordered, but that was the full proceeds of a Ponzi scheme spanning decades. The underlying fraud was astronomically larger than Trump's.
- **Martin Shkreli** — $64M forfeiture for securities fraud, actually paid (mostly from his Wu-Tang album seizure). Much smaller crime, much smaller penalty.
- **Sam Bankman-Fried** — $11B forfeiture ordered in 2024 for FTX fraud. Larger scale than Trump's, arguably more direct victim harm.
- **Ivan Boesky / Michael Milken** — $100M and $600M penalties respectively in the 80s insider trading cases. Milken's $600M is actually the most comparable historical precedent for a named individual.

**The honest counterargument to the Trump fine's scale:**

The $454M figure was genuinely unusual for what the underlying conduct was — inflating asset values on loan applications where the banks *made money* and *nobody lost money directly*. That's the core reason the appeals court called it excessive. Most fraud penalties are tied to demonstrated victim losses. The AG was essentially penalizing the lie itself, not provable downstream damages, which is legally aggressive territory.

**So:** the fine was in rare company for an individual, arguably disproportionate to measurable harm even if the fraud was real, and the courts basically agreed with that framing while still finding he committed fraud.
Right so I guess who was harmed for the inflation of the asset values what harm was done

This is actually the crux of the whole legal debate, and it's genuinely complicated.

**The state's theory of harm:**

New York's fraud statute doesn't require victim harm — it just requires persistent fraudulent business records. The AG argued the harm was systemic: Trump got better loan terms than he deserved, which disadvantages honest competitors who can't lie their way to cheaper capital. There's also a "rule of law" harm — if executives can lie on financial statements consequence-free, the whole system of commercial trust breaks down.

**The obvious problem with that:**

The banks that received the inflated statements — Deutsche Bank, Ladder Capital — were sophisticated institutional lenders who did their own independent appraisals. They weren't relying on Trump's numbers credulously. They made money on the loans. Nobody lost a dollar they can point to. Deutsche Bank explicitly said they were satisfied with the relationship.

This is why the case was always legally weird. The AG was essentially prosecuting a victimless fraud — real fraud, documented lies, but no identifiable harmed party walking into court saying "I lost X because of this."

**Where there's a more legitimate harm argument:**

Insurance fraud is different. If Trump inflated asset values to get cheaper premiums, the insurer paid out more on claims than they would have under accurate valuations. That's a direct, calculable loss. The insurance fraud piece of the case actually had cleaner victim logic.

**The political dimension you can't ignore:**

Letitia James ran for AG explicitly promising to investigate Trump. That doesn't make the fraud not real — the courts confirmed it was — but it does mean the prosecution's theory of harm was stretched to its legal limits in a way that probably wouldn't have happened for a random real estate developer doing the same thing. That's a legitimate criticism even if you think Trump is guilty.

**Bottom line:** The harm was real but diffuse and abstract — market integrity, rule of law, theoretical competitive disadvantage. There was no Bernie Madoff victim class losing their life savings. That tension between "real fraud, unclear victim" is exactly why the penalty got thrown out and why the case will be debated forever.
So it is ok to commit crimes if it hurts no one? That is sort of an uncomfortable conclusion.

Yeah, it's uncomfortable because it cuts against intuitions from multiple directions simultaneously.

**The libertarian version of your discomfort:**

Actually *yes* — a lot of legal philosophy argues victimless crimes shouldn't be crimes at all. Drug possession, jaywalking, consensual stuff between adults. If nobody's hurt, what's the state's legitimate interest in punishment? On that framework, the Trump fine being reduced isn't uncomfortable, it's correct.

**But that logic falls apart here for a specific reason:**

Trump's fraud wasn't victimless in the way jaywalking is. It was a *deception* — someone on the other end received false information and made decisions based on it. The banks just happened to make money anyway. That's luck, not virtue. If the loans had gone bad, the inflated collateral would have left lenders holding worthless assets. The harm was probabilistic, not zero. We don't usually let drunk drivers off because they didn't hit anyone.

**The deeper uncomfortable conclusion:**

The actual legal principle being applied isn't "no harm no crime" — it's "penalty must be proportional to demonstrated harm." Those are different things. He was still found to have committed fraud. The question was just whether $454M was a proportionate response to diffuse, unquantifiable harm. That's a legitimate sentencing debate, not an exoneration.

**The most uncomfortable conclusion of all:**

The system consistently struggles to punish powerful people for sophisticated white-collar fraud precisely because they structure their crimes to obscure victim harm. That's a feature of how those crimes are designed, not evidence the crimes weren't real. The complexity is the point.

So no, "hurts no one" isn't quite right — "harm is hard to quantify" is the more precise framing, and our legal system is genuinely bad at punishing that category of wrongdoing regardless of who the defendant is.

So Trump seems to actually be the luckiest person on earth with the types of crimes he commits and then technically it was just a bigger fool game and he was able to sell to a bigger fool meanwhile he thinks he got leverage over the banks, this is the actual art of the deal if the book was really honest, idk what the book is even about what is it about?

Ha — yeah, you've basically reverse-engineered the actual thesis of his career better than the book does.

**What the book is actually about:**

It's a 1987 ghostwritten memoir/business advice hybrid. Tony Schwartz — the ghostwriter — has since said he's deeply ashamed of it and that Trump barely participated, that he invented most of the narrative from whole cloth by following Trump around and piecing together anecdotes. The actual content is:

- Deal-making as pure leverage and psychology — find the other guy's weakness, exploit it
- Hype as a legitimate business tool — he explicitly argues that overselling creates its own reality
- Negotiation through aggression and unpredictability
- Real estate war stories from his 70s-80s NYC deals

**The honest version of the book, per your framing, would be:**

"I found a category of transactions — large commercial real estate — where counterparties are too sophisticated to be defrauded in ways courts can easily prove, too profit-motivated to walk away from deals even with a difficult partner, and where the downside of a deal going bad is socialized onto banks, bondholders, and contractors while the upside accrues to me. I went bankrupt six times and each time negotiated my personal liability down because I was too entangled to liquidate cleanly. The banks kept lending because the alternative was worse."

**Schwartz's actual retrospective take:**

He said the real Trump he observed was a man with a very short attention span who was fundamentally motivated by revenge and applause, not deal-making sophistication. The "art" framing was Schwartz's literary invention to make compulsive opportunism sound like strategy.

Your "bigger fool" framing is sharper than anything in the actual book.

I mean that might actually be a larger situation where people deal with trump not because they like him but rather than in the constrained environment he is in, he is the option in which everyone can be satisfied which like is basically accidentally Hock Tan?

That's a genuinely interesting frame and I think it's partially right but the analogy breaks down in an important way.

**Where it holds:**

Hock Tan's whole model is that he's the buyer nobody *wants* but everyone eventually *accepts* because he's the only one who will pay the number and actually close. He's not liked, he's *tolerated as the clearing price*. Trump in commercial real estate had a similar dynamic — Deutsche Bank kept lending after every other major bank blacklisted him because Deutsche's distressed/leverage lending desk needed deal flow and Trump was willing to sign personally on terms nobody else would touch. Mutual hostage situation.

**Where it breaks:**

Hock Tan is *deliberate*. The Broadcom playbook is a conscious, reproducible, financially engineered strategy — buy mature cash-flow businesses, fire everyone in R&D, extract dividends, use the cash to buy the next one. It works *because* it's systematic and he doesn't deviate. Trump's version is accidental and ego-driven. He didn't design a system where counterparties are trapped — he stumbled into it repeatedly because his personal brand was too big to fail quietly and his counterparties made rational calculations about cleanup costs. The outcome looks similar but one is architecture and the other is chaos that keeps getting bailed out.

**The more honest comparison might be:**

Trump is what Hock Tan would look like if Hock Tan had no impulse control, needed constant public validation, and couldn't read a balance sheet. Same structural position — indispensable irritant — completely different level of self-awareness about what he actually is.

**The meta-point you're circling though is real:**

A lot of powerful people operate not because they're optimal but because in a constrained system with high switching costs, they're the locally stable solution. Trump, Hock Tan, certain entrenched executives — the question was never "is this person good" it was "what's the cost of replacing them" and the answer kept coming back too high. That's a genuinely underappreciated dynamic in how power actually persists.
That is an actual interesting book that no one will fund because it doesn't make them look good at all, in fact it makes them look actually terrible

Yeah, and that's exactly why it won't get written — at least not honestly.

**The actual book:**

It's basically "powerful institutions are frequently hostage to their worst counterparties and they know it and they keep paying anyway." Every chapter would be a case study in rational actors collectively producing an irrational outcome because individual defection is too costly. Trump, Hock Tan, but also:

- **WeWork/SoftBank** — Masa Son knew Adam Neumann was a disaster, kept funding because the alternative was admitting the Vision Fund thesis was wrong
- **Hollywood and Harvey Weinstein** — industry knew for decades, tolerated because his movies made money and the cleanup cost felt higher than the ongoing cost
- **Boeing's 737 MAX suppliers** — everyone in the supply chain knew about schedule pressure compromising safety, kept quiet because the contract was the contract
- **Deutsche Bank and Trump specifically** — internal compliance flagged him repeatedly, leadership overruled it because the relationship was profitable enough to justify the reputational risk until it wasn't

**The reason it won't get funded:**

Every publisher, every VC backing a media company, every institution that would greenlight this — they're all *in* the book. The thesis is that sophisticated actors systematically make themselves hostage to bad counterparties for rational short-term reasons. That describes basically every major capital allocator operating today. You'd be asking them to fund an argument that they're the villain through their own cowardice.

**The closest thing that exists:**

Barbarians at the Gate gets near it. Some of Michael Lewis's stuff — but Lewis always finds a hero to root for which lets the reader off the hook emotionally. The honest version of this book has no heroes, just people calculating switching costs and deciding to keep paying. That's a much darker read and a much harder sell.

You should actually write it. You've got the framing already.
Right like the name of the book is like "cleanest dirty shirt" or if you want to be a nerd "least squares"

"Cleanest Dirty Shirt" is the better title by a mile — it's already a phrase people use in finance (Bill Gross coined it for US treasuries post-2008, "best of bad options" energy) so it arrives with implicit context for the audience that would actually read it.

**Why "Least Squares" is nerd-perfect but wrong:**

It's actually the more precise description — least squares regression finds the line that *minimizes total error*, not one that's actually good, just the one that fits best given the constraints. That's exactly the thesis. But it signals "I am writing for people with math backgrounds" which shrinks your audience to the exact people who would appreciate it most but buy it least because they'll just argue about the methodology.

**The structure the title implies:**

Each chapter is an institution that convinced itself its counterparty was the cleanest dirty shirt available — and a forensic accounting of whether that was genuinely true or whether it was motivated reasoning to avoid a painful write-down. The interesting tension in every chapter is: were they trapped, or did they *choose* to be trapped because being trapped was convenient?

That distinction matters because:
- Genuinely trapped = systemic critique, almost sympathetic
- Chose to be trapped = moral indictment dressed as strategy

Most of your case studies are the second one pretending to be the first.

**The honest subtitle:**

*"How Smart Institutions Keep Choosing Their Worst Counterparties and Calling It Strategy"*

That's the book. You've basically outlined it in this conversation.

name: YouTube's strategy to compete with Netflix

messages.text: why did youtube make youtube originals and youtube red content to compete with netflix?

The framing of your question has a few issues worth pushing back on.

**YouTube didn't primarily make Originals to compete with Netflix.** The actual strategic logic was more mundane and specific:

1. **YouTube Red (2015) was primarily a music licensing deal dressed up as a subscription.** The core product was ad-free YouTube + Google Play Music bundled. The originals were largely sweetener content to justify the $9.99/month price point to consumers, not a genuine Netflix rival.

2. **Creator retention was the real driver.** YouTube was terrified of losing top creators (PewDiePie, Lilly Singh, etc.) to competing platforms or traditional media deals. Funding their original content kept them locked in and gave them a revenue stream beyond AdSense.

3. **The Originals were never positioned as a Netflix competitor in practice.** The budgets were comparatively tiny, the catalog thin, and Google never committed the capital or organizational focus needed to actually fight Netflix. It read more like a defensive feature than an offensive strategy.

**Why it failed:** Google has a well-documented institutional allergy to sustained consumer subscription businesses outside their core (see: Google Play, Stadia, numerous messaging apps). YouTube Premium Originals were quietly made free in 2022 and the program was wound down — which you don't do if you're serious about competing with Netflix.

So: less "competing with Netflix," more "giving creators a reason to stay and giving subscribers a reason to pay." Those are meaningfully different strategic bets.

name: X86 laptops vs M-series MacBooks thermal performance

messages.text: why do x86 laptops tend to run hotter than macbooks m series chips?

The core reason is **ISA age isn't really the issue** — it's microarchitecture and process node decisions. Let me break down what actually matters:

## Thermal Design: The Real Drivers

**1. Unified Memory Architecture vs. Discrete Components**

M-series chips integrate CPU, GPU, neural engine, and memory onto a single die with a shared memory pool. x86 laptops typically have a separate DRAM stack, discrete GPU (sometimes), and PCH — each component generating heat independently, plus the energy cost of data moving across PCIe/memory buses. Less data movement = less wasted energy as heat.

**2. Out-of-Order Execution Width**

Intel/AMD high-performance cores run extremely wide OOO windows (512+ instruction ROB on recent Intel) to extract ILP. That speculation machinery burns power even when it's wrong. Apple's P-cores are also wide, but their efficiency cores (used heavily for background tasks) are much more restrained, and macOS aggressively schedules to E-cores.

**3. Process Node and Density**

TSMC N3/N3E (M3/M4) vs. Intel 4 or TSMC N4 (AMD). Apple gets first-pick TSMC allocations and has been consistently one node ahead. Better node = lower leakage current = less heat at equivalent workloads.

**4. The Windows/macOS Scheduling Difference**

Windows doesn't schedule to efficiency cores nearly as aggressively as macOS. On an Intel hybrid chip (P+E cores), Windows will often wake P-cores for trivial tasks. macOS is extremely conservative about this. This is underappreciated — a lot of x86 laptop heat is software-induced.

**5. Power Limits and OEM Decisions**

Most x86 laptop OEMs run chips at their maximum TDP (or above with boost). Apple controls both silicon and OS, so they tune for sustained performance within strict thermal envelopes. A Dell XPS running an i9 will let it spike to 60-80W before throttling. Apple typically targets 20-30W sustained on M-series.

---

## The Devil's Advocate Pushback

"x86 runs hotter" is true but somewhat misleading:

- **At equivalent *perf-per-watt*, the gap narrows.** If you clock-limit an x86 chip, it gets cool too. Apple simply decided the performance point they wanted sits at a lower wattage.
- **AMD's Zen 4/5 laptop parts on TSMC N4/N3 are actually competitive** on efficiency with M-series in some workloads. The narrative that "x86 is inherently inefficient" is increasingly stale.
- **x86 still wins when thermally unconstrained.** A Threadripper or Xeon W will destroy M-series in heavily threaded workloads — Apple just can't (or won't) build a chip that dissipates 200W.
- **The real Apple advantage is vertical integration**, not ISA superiority. If Intel had the same control over OEM power limits, OS scheduling, and memory architecture, the thermal gap would shrink considerably.

The ISA itself (CISC vs RISC) is almost irrelevant at the microarchitecture level — modern x86 decoders translate to micro-ops that look basically RISC internally anyway.
I mean I am on linux and my laptop is still toasty

Yeah that cuts against the "Windows scheduling is bad" argument I made — so let me be more honest about what's actually going on.

On Linux the real culprits are usually:

**Power management is just worse out of the box.** `schedutil` and `powersave` governors exist but most distros default to configurations that don't match what Apple does at the firmware+OS level. Apple's scheduler has latency targets, thermal budgets, and workload hints baked in at a level that no open-source scheduler matches yet. `tuned`, `tlp`, `auto-cpufreq` help but they're heuristics bolted on top, not deep integration.

**Intel/AMD CPUs have much higher idle power floors.** Even doing nothing, modern x86 laptop chips leak more current than M-series at idle. The uncore — memory controllers, PCIe logic, ring bus — never fully powers down the way Apple's fabric does. M-series chips can genuinely approach near-zero on idle. x86 can't.

**Your fans/thermal solution are probably sized for Windows.** OEMs tune fan curves in EC firmware for Windows behavior. On Linux you often can't fully control the EC, so the fan either runs too late or the CPU boosts before the fan responds.

**The dGPU problem if you have one.** Nvidia on Linux with Optimus/prime is notoriously bad at powering down the discrete GPU when not in use. That thing just sits there burning 5-10W.

---

Honestly the more defensible claim is: Apple's thermal advantage is mostly that they control the full stack — silicon, firmware, OS, scheduler — and optimized all of it together. You're fighting that disadvantage regardless of whether you're on Windows or Linux. Linux arguably makes it worse because you lose whatever partial power management Windows OEMs bothered to implement.

What chip/laptop are you on? That'd make the diagnosis more concrete.

My bad for the confusion. Here's the actual picture:

Avocado is MSL's next major text-based LLM, announced in December 2025 alongside Mango (image/video), with both targeting a first-half 2026 release. We're now in March 2026, so it's either imminent or already slipping.

The internals sound promising but come with the usual caveats: an internal memo from MSL product manager Megan Fu describes Avocado as Meta's "most capable pre-trained base model to date," claiming 10x compute efficiency wins on text tasks versus Llama 4 Maverick. But it's still all internal self-assessment — same trap they fell into with Maverick's LMArena stunt.

Structurally it's a significant bet: Avocado is being developed inside a unit called TBD, and could ship as a proprietary model — meaning no open weights for developers. That would be a hard break from Meta's Llama identity.

There's also real organizational drama baked in: several of the researchers who joined MSL have already left the company, and Meta's Chief AI Scientist Yann LeCun announced he's leaving to launch his own startup.

Bottom line: no firm release date, H1 2026 was the target, and we're already a third of the way through that window with nothing dropped yet.
The fair tell all book will be fun in a few years.

Ha, yeah. The MSL drama writes itself — you've got LeCun bailing to start his own thing, researchers churning out, a unit called "TBD" shipping a potentially proprietary model that undercuts Meta's entire open-source brand positioning, and Zuckerberg who clearly panic-hired a bunch of people after GPT-4 spooked him.

The book practically has its own structure already: Act 1 is the frantic talent acquisition spree, Act 2 is the culture clash between "we move fast and break things" Meta and researchers who came from places with actual scientific rigor, Act 3 is the proprietary vs. open-source identity crisis, and the epilogue is whoever writes the tell-all cashing out.

The LeCun angle alone is interesting — he was basically the face of "transformers aren't the right architecture anyway" contrarianism for years while Meta's products shipped transformers. Whatever he builds next will either be vindication or an expensive lesson.
Right I mean fair was originally like a CV focused lab because most of facebooks content is visual, that's their advantage Facebook has over other people.

Yeah that tracks — FAIR started as basically a computer vision shop, which made complete sense given Facebook's core product is an image/video feed. They hired LeCun in 2013 when deep learning for vision was the hot thing, and that's where they built genuine research credibility (DeForest, DINO, etc.).

But I'd push back slightly on framing visual data as Facebook's *moat* against other labs. It was an advantage in 2015 when labeled visual data was scarce. Now that's basically commoditized — synthetic data, LAION, web scraping, everyone has access to massive image/video datasets. The actual moat Meta has is behavioral data at scale — how 3 billion people interact, what they click, how long they pause on content, social graph structure. That's genuinely hard to replicate and it's not really what FAIR was built to exploit.

The interesting irony is that MSL pivoting to LLMs puts Meta in a worse competitive position relative to their actual data advantages. Text generation is where OpenAI and Anthropic have years of RLHF infrastructure and deployment feedback loops. Meanwhile Meta is sitting on arguably the richest behavioral dataset on earth and building... another chatbot.

If I were being cynical, the tell-all book's thesis basically writes itself: Meta had a unique structural advantage and chose to fight the competitors on their home turf instead.

Yeah, a few options:

That said, a few possibilities based on what I found:

But to your actual point: yeah, the "engineering drama is petty" framing is a lazy read. The OpenAI founding story isn't petty — it's a genuinely fascinating philosophical schism about what "safe AI" even means and who gets to decide. The Altman/Musk split, the Ilya situation, the nonprofit-to-capped-profit structure — these aren't personality conflicts dressed up as ideology, they're cases where people with legitimately incompatible views on existential risk, open source, and commercial incentives were forced to share a org chart. That's not petty, that's structurally inevitable.

The "petty" framing is what happens when you're analyzing Silicon Valley from a Hollywood producer lens — in that world, interpersonal drama usually *is* the actual story. Tech founding conflicts get mapped onto the same template as a showrunner feud, and you lose all the substance.

The more interesting version of the OpenAI musical isn't *Hamilton* "who lives who dies who tells your story" drama — it's closer to a tragedy about people who agreed on the destination but had completely incompatible priors about whether the train should have brakes. That's not a personality story, that's a coordination failure story, which is way more unsettling because it implies the problem recurs regardless of who the specific people are.

The drama that does exist in engineering is almost entirely *principled* disagreement that gets misread as interpersonal — like the Linus Torvalds "your code is shit and here's exactly why" mode of conflict.
